tasks.name: Women’s Health Primary Care Practitioners and Attrition From Veterans Health Administration Care

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**Outlet**: U.S. Medicine

**Reporter: **LaTina Emerson

**Subject: **Women’s Health Primary Care Practitioners and
Attrition From Veterans Health Administration Care

**Deadline:** 4/17

**Background:** This query is requesting responses to questions about
the study: “Women’s Health Primary Care Practitioners and Attrition from
Veterans Health Administration Care” which Dr. Jessica Breland, Dr. Elizabeth
Yano and Dr. Alison Hamilton of the Palo Alto VAMC is listed among the
co-authors. The reporter would like VA SME to respond to the query.

**Questions:**

**Q1: Can you furtherdiscuss the VA’s efforts to correct historical gaps in care for women, such asconcentrating relevant expertise among designated women’s health primary carepractitioners and offering trainings, such as the Women’s Health Mini-Residency?What information is taught the Women’s Health Mini-Residency? Have theseefforts been successful so far? Does the VA have any additional plans tocorrect historical gaps in care for women?**

**A1:** VA has addressed ways to improve women’s health care
by offering women Veterans assignment to designated Women’s Health Primary Care
Providers (PCPs), who are specifically trained or experienced in women’s health
care. Evaluations show this model improves satisfaction and quality of
women-specific care, supported by initiatives such as Women Veteran Program
Managers, gynecology workforce expansion, care coordination, facility site
visits, Women’s Health Innovations & Staffing Enhancements (WHISE), and the
Women’s Health Mini-Residency trainings. Over 14,000 clinicians have completed
Mini- Residency training since 2008, strengthening nationwide access to
high-quality, comprehensive women’s health care and environments.

*A1 NOTE: Could just focus on what happens in the mini-residency... what’staught, how has that spread throughout VA and helped women Veterans?*

**Q2: If you were aclinician, how would you use the results of this study in your practice? Shouldall VHA clinicians (both male and female) aim to offer better care to womenpatients and be required to participate in trainings, if additional skills areneeded?**

**A2:** Given that women who see women’s health PCPs are more
likely to continue using VHA care, other providers who aren’t women’s health
PCPs may also want to expand their knowledge of women Veterans’ health care
through training and consultation with subject matter experts developed within
the system.

*A2 NOTE: Seems to suggest some VA facilities might not worried aboutwomen Veterans. Is there a more succinct answer?*

**Q3: How do these resultschange practice for physicians?**

**A3:** Since our results highlight how important women’s
health care is to women Veterans, we think the results could inspire to
clinicians who are currently women’s health PCPs and might attract even more
clinicians to want to serve women Veterans in this way.  Our work also
shows a measure of effectiveness of this important model of care for VA, and
physicians outside VA might want to pursue similar care models.

NOTE: While researchers don’t
dictate how a physician treats a patient, we hope the information in this study
contributes to a greater understanding of causes and that leads to better
overall care.

*A3 NOTE: Please re-write. Is there anything in the study suggesting a wayVA practitioners should adjust how they work? Or if they do X, Y, Z, they canget better outcomes.*

**Q4: What is the mostimportant finding(s) about this study that our audience (healthcareprofessionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military HealthSystem and the Indian Health Service) should know?**

**A4:** We found that women new to VA health care who see a
women’s health PCP are much less likely to leave VHA care. Women’s health PCPs
have proficiency through training and experience, as well as a desire to
deliver women’s health care. So, while we don’t know the specific mechanism for
our results, women’s health PCP care may help women Veterans want to keep using
VHA.

*A4 NOTE: Does the study have numbers showing that women who see a women’shealth PCP are less likely to leave VHA care?*

**Q5: What situation causedyou to want to do this study?**

**A5:** We were drawn to this work because previous data
suggested that women new to VA health care were more likely to leave VHA care.
To help advance care for women Veterans, we wanted to see whether women’s
health PCPs might provide better experiences and help reduce attrition for
women Veterans in VHA.

**Q6: Is there anything thatyou’d like to add?**

**A6:** Women Veterans are VA’s fastest growing group of
health care users. Among the more than one million women Veterans currently
enrolled in VA health care, approximately 72% choose to use VA health care and
benefits from the VA Office of Women’s Health initiatives, designed and
sustained to meet women Veterans’ evolving, life-stage-based health and care
needs.

tasks.name: Docetaxel Rechallenge vs Cabazitaxel in Patients With Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
Source/SME: Karlynn N. Dulberger, PhD; Associate Director of Data Science, Boston CSP Informatics Center, VA Boston Healthcare System
Query Submitted: 3/02
Deadline: 3/10
Study link: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41543856/
Outlet: U.S. Medicine
Reporter: Mary Anne Dunkin

**The researchers would likethe following note included:**

This work was conducted as
part of the VA Partnership for Real-world Outcomes, Findings and Observations
Using National Data on Veterans (PROFOUND-VET) initiative, which is part of the
Office of Research and Development Precision Oncology Actively Managed
Portfolio. PROFOUND-VET’s mission is to establish gold standards for VA
real-world cancer data analysis to inform clinical guidelines, pathways, and
trials.

**Q1: What clinical uncertainty or real-world practice pattern prompted you tocompare docetaxel rechallenge with cabazitaxel in this patient population?**

**A1: **In clinical practice, some patients with metastatic
castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) stop docetaxel for
reasons other than disease progression, such as planned treatment breaks or
cumulative toxicity. In these situations, clinicians vary in whether they
pursue a docetaxel rechallenge or switch to cabazitaxel. Because the
comparative effectiveness of these two approaches had not been well defined, our
study sought to address the uncertainty about which option might be most
beneficial for this specific patient population.

**Q2: You specificallyincluded patients who did not experience disease progression during theirinitial course of docetaxel. Why was that distinction critical to your studydesign?**

**A2:** Progression during the initial course of docetaxel
generally indicates that the cancer has developed resistance mechanisms. In
those cases, retreatment with docetaxel would not be expected to provide
benefit and would expose patients to toxicity without therapeutic value. To
meaningfully evaluate a rechallenge strategy, it was essential to focus on
patients who had not progressed during their first course and therefore might
retain docetaxel sensitivity.

**Q3: Your study found amedian overall survival of 12.3 months with docetaxel rechallenge compared with9.6 months with cabazitaxel. How should clinicians interpret that difference inpractical terms?**

**A3:** Median overall survival differences between single‑agent
therapies for mCRPC are typically modest. The difference observed here falls
within the range seen with other approved agents. While individual patients may
experience outcomes better or worse than the median, these results suggest that
rechallenging with docetaxel may be a reasonable option for appropriately
selected patients. The findings provide additional context but do not replace an
individual clinician’s decision‑making based on the patient’s specific medical
factors.

**Q4: Cabazitaxel wasdeveloped to overcome taxane resistance. How do you explain the possibilitythat rechallenging with docetaxel may perform better in certain patients?**

**A4: **Our study specifically focused on patients who had not
progressed during their initial course of docetaxel. Because these individuals
were more likely to retain sensitivity to docetaxel, it is plausible that
rechallenging with docetaxel could perform favorably in this particular group.

**Q5: How should cliniciansweigh these retrospective Veterans Affairs data against prospective clinicaltrial data supporting cabazitaxel in the post-docetaxel setting?**

**A5: **Our findings add contextual information for clinicians
when considering the full range of available evidence. Prospective randomized
controlled trials remain the gold standard for establishing treatment efficacy.
In circumstances where such trials are unlikely due to feasibility or resource
constraints, real‑world comparative effectiveness studies can supplement the
evidence base by providing insights into how treatments perform in routine
clinical practice.

**Q6: Even with inverseprobability of treatment weighting, what unmeasured factors could still haveinfluenced which patients received rechallenge versus cabazitaxel?**

**A6: **Unmeasured or unavailable clinical factors, such as
disease volume, symptom burden, or other details not captured in the record,
may still have influenced which treatment was selected. Even with efforts to
account for known differences between groups, it is always possible for
observational studies to be affected by variables not included in the data.

**Q7: Based on your study,are there changes you would recommend for future U.S. lung cancer screeningguidelines to better address disparities?**

**A7: **Our study focused on treatment for metastatic
castration‑resistant prostate cancer and did not evaluate lung cancer
screening. As a result, we cannot draw conclusions or make recommendations
regarding future lung cancer screening guidelines based on these data.

**Q8: Based on yourfindings, how should oncologists approach treatment sequencing for patientswith mCRPC who previously responded to docetaxel?**

**Revised A8:**

For patients who previously responded to docetaxel, these retrospective
findings suggest that a docetaxel rechallenge may be a reasonable option to
consider alongside other available therapies. Treatment sequencing should
continue to be guided by clinical judgment, patient preferences, comorbidities,
and the broader body of evidence.

**Q9: Do these findingsjustify a prospective randomized trial, or do you believe the current evidenceis sufficient to influence practice?**

**Revised A9:**

A prospective randomized trial would provide the most definitive comparison,
but such a study may be difficult to conduct given the required scale and the
evolving therapeutic landscape. In the absence of prospective data,
retrospective analyses like ours can help inform clinical decision‑making,
although they should be interpreted in the context of their methodological
limitations.

tasks.name: Temporal Trends of Common Cardiac Surgical Procedures at Veterans Affairs Medical Centers

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**Source**: Elizabeth Y. Wang, MD; cardiothoracic surgeon at the
Veterans Affairs Palo Alto Health Care System

**Query Submitted: **3/02

**Deadline: **3/11

**Study**: [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41670963/](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fpubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2F41670963%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7C84307331e6da4a285f5908de7ee10b6e%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C639087702183920310%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=xFKFske1BMwHaGMSTquabRcooUETzZRZgfE4bl2V1fk%3D&reserved=0)

**Outlet: **U.S. Medicine

**Reporter**: Mary Anne Dunkin

**Q1: What motivated you toexamine long-term trends in cardiac surgery volume and outcomes within VAmedical centers at this particular moment?**

**A1: **We wanted to better understand how cardiac surgery has
evolved within the VA system over the past two decades. Cardiovascular care has
changed substantially during this time due to advances in interventional
cardiology, the COVID-19 pandemic, and policies such as the VA MISSION Act,
which expanded access to community care. To assess the impact, we reviewed
94,694 cardiac surgeries across 43 VA medical centers between 2005 and 2024 to
examine trends in volume, patient complexity, and outcomes.

**Q2: You observed thatcardiac surgery volumes peaked in 2006 and declined after 2019 beforestabilizing. What do you think is driving those shifts?**

**A2: **The peak in 2006 followed by a gradual decline likely
reflects broader national trends. Improvements in medical therapy and the
increasing use of less invasive procedures, such as PCI and transcatheter valve
therapies, have reduced the need for some open cardiac operations. The
subsequent decline after 2019 followed the COVID-19 pandemic, as well as the
implementation of the VA MISSION Act, which expanded access to community care
and shifted cardiac cases outside the VA system. Together, these factors account
for both the long-term and recent declines.

**Q3: Over the 20-yearperiod, patients became older and had higher comorbidity burdens. Howsignificant was that increase in clinical complexity?**

**A3: **The increase in clinical complexity was substantial.
The mean age of Veterans undergoing cardiac surgery increased from 65 years in
2005–2009 to 71 years in 2020–2024. Over the same period, the mean age-adjusted
Charlson Comorbidity Index—which considers major chronic conditions to estimate
a patient’s overall medical risk— increased from 4 to 6.1. This indicated a
higher burden of chronic conditions, such as congestive heart failure, chronic
kidney disease, peripheral vascular disease, and diabetes with complications.

**Q4: Despite increasingcomorbidity and changing volumes, adjusted 30-day mortality remainedconsistent. How do you interpret that finding?**

**A4: **This is an encouraging finding. Despite treating a
more medically complex population over time, risk-adjusted 30-day mortality
remained stable across the study period. This suggests that the quality
monitoring and care coordination within the VA system have helped maintain
outcomes.

**Q5: There’s longstandingdebate about the relationship between surgical volume and outcomes. What doyour findings suggest about quality of cardiac surgery within the VA system?**

**A5: **Our findings suggest that VA cardiac surgery programs
continue to achieve strong outcomes even as case volumes have declined. While
the relationship between procedural volume and outcomes remains important, the
stability of risk-adjusted mortality in our analysis suggests that VA cardiac
surgery programs have maintained consistent quality. This likely reflects the
strengths of an integrated healthcare system with robust quality monitoring
through programs such as the VA Surgical Quality Improvement Program (VASQIP).

**Q6: How might theimplementation of the VA MISSION Act — which expanded access to community care— affect cardiac surgery volume and referral patterns within the VA system?**

**A6: **The MISSION Act expanded Veterans’ access to care
outside the VA under certain circumstances, which may have contributed to the
decline in VA cardiac surgery volumes after 2019. As more Veterans gain access
to community providers, some procedures that historically occurred within the
VA may now be performed in community hospitals. Understanding how these
referral patterns evolve will be important for planning the future structure
and capacity of VA cardiac surgery programs.

**Q7: Your study used VASQIPdata and age-adjusted Charlson Comorbidity Index for risk adjustment. What arethe key limitations readers should keep in mind?**

**A7: **Our analysis includes only procedures performed within
VA medical centers and captured in VASQIP. Surgeries performed in community
hospitals under VA-funded care were not included, so the data do not represent
all cardiac surgeries performed for Veterans. In addition, while the
age-adjusted Charlson Comorbidity Index provides a useful measure of overall
comorbidity burden and has been validated in recent cardiac surgery studies, it
is not a procedure-specific risk model.

**Q8: What should Veterans,cardiologists, and referring physicians take away from these findings whendeciding where cardiac surgery should be performed?**

**A8: **The key takeaway is that cardiac surgery performed
within the VA system continues to achieve strong outcomes even as the patient
population has become older and more medically complex. Some recent studies
examining care after implementation of the VA MISSION Act suggest that while
expanded community care improved access, outcomes for some cardiovascular
procedures were more favorable when surgery was performed within VA medical
centers. Taken together, these findings suggest that decisions about where surgery
should occur should balance patient needs and access while recognizing the
consistently high-quality care available within the VA.

**Background:** This query is requesting responses to questions about
the study T[emporal Trends, Setting, and Timing of Palliative and Hospice
Care in COPD in the Veterans Health Administration, 2010-2020](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.chestpulmonary.org%2Farticle%2FS2949-7892%2825%2900038-8%2Ffulltext&data=05%7C02%7C%7C096f1c1a6f8c49cc8ff908de5d16fd3b%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C639050550490834569%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=XkLTJFCGVWNbuYspCaCOwZ6XC2HJUPxVyPS0%2BRBDEDs%3D&reserved=0).  Drs.
Robert Plumley, Center of Innovation for Veteran-Centered and Value-Driven

Affairs-Birmingham/Atlanta Geriatric Research and David Au Veteran-Centered and
Value-Driven Care are listed as co-authors on this study. The reporter would
like one or more VA SMEs to respond to the query.

Could you please send written
responses by **COB Friday, Jan. 30**? If you have any questions or need
clarification, please let me know. I’m happy to help.

Here are the questions:

**A1: **

tasks.name: Social Determinants of Health and Suicide Risk in U.S. Military Veterans

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**Approving Official(s): **Matthew Davio, ORD Communications Chief

**Attribution**: Robert H. Pietrzak, PhD, MPH; Research Psychologist
in the Clinical Neurosciences Division at the VA National Center for PTSD

**Outlet**: U.S. Medicine

**Subject: **Social Determinants of Health and Suicide Risk in U.S.
Military Veterans

**Deadline: **1/13

**Contact Info:** M. Dunkin madunkin@gmail.com

**Background:** This query is requesting responses to questions about
the study: **Social Determinants of Health and Suicide Risk in U.S. MilitaryVeterans **which Dr. Robert H. Pietrzak and Dr. John Krstal of the New
England VAMC are listed among the co-authors. The reporter would like one or
both VA SMEs to respond to the query. 

Q1: **What prompted you tostudy the social determinants of health and suicide risk in Veterans?**

A1: We became increasingly
aware many Veterans experiencing suicidal thoughts or behaviors were also
facing substantial social adversity. Prior research has often examined
individual social factors in isolation, but we sought to better understand how
the combined burden of multiple adverse social determinants of health (SDoH)
contribute to suicide risk among Veterans, beyond well-established clinical
predictors such as psychiatric disorders.

Q2: **How did you go aboutconducting this study?**

A2: We analyzed data from the
National Health and Resilience in Veterans Study, which is a nationally
representative survey of more than 4,000 U.S. Veterans, including those engaged
and not engaged in VA healthcare. Using 21 indicators from Healthy People 2030
and related social determinants of health frameworks, we developed a composite
measure spanning five key domains: economic conditions, trauma burden,
healthcare access, neighborhood environment, and social context. We then
applied weighted logistic regression models to examine how cumulative SDoH
burden, and specific SDoH factors, were associated with suicidal thoughts and
behaviors.

Q3: **What were your keyfindings?**

A3: We observed a strong,
graded association between cumulative adverse SDoH and suicidal thoughts and
behaviors. Even after adjusting for a wide range of demographic, military, and
health-related factors, each standard deviation increase in SDoH burden was
linked to more than double the odds of past-year suicidal ideations and future
suicidal intent, as well as over 70% higher odds of lifetime suicide attempts.
Veterans in the highest 5% of SDoH burden were more than 20 times more likely
to report suicidal thoughts or behaviors compared with those in the lowest 5%.
These associations were especially pronounced among Veterans with a history of
psychiatric diagnoses.

Q4: **Did any of thefindings surprise you?**

A4: The magnitude and clear
“dose-response” pattern of the associations were particularly striking. Even
after accounting for demographic, military, and clinical factors, the
cumulative impact of social disadvantage emerged as a powerful predictor of suicidal
thoughts and behaviors. The degree to which risk escalated among Veterans
facing multiple concurrent social stressors — especially those with psychiatric
histories — underscores how strongly social conditions can compound
vulnerability to suicide.

Q5: **What do you think arethe implications of your findings?**

A5: These results suggest
that suicide prevention cannot rely solely on clinical interventions or crisis
response. Addressing upstream social risk factors -- such as housing
instability, financial strain, limited access to care, trauma burden, and
social isolation -- may be essential components of a comprehensive prevention
strategy. Our findings also support routine screening for adverse SDoH in both
VA and community-based settings, paired with stronger referral pathways to
social and supportive services.

Q6: **What is the mostimportant takeaway for our readers, most of whom are clinicians in the VA?**

A6: The key message is that
social conditions matter profoundly for suicide risk among Veterans. Those
experiencing multiple social disadvantages are at exceptionally high risk, even
beyond traditional clinical indicators. Integrating greater awareness of social
determinants into routine care, alongside evidence-based mental health
treatment, may help clinicians identify vulnerable Veterans earlier and
intervene more effectively. More broadly, improving the social environments
where Veterans live, work, and age could help save lives.

tasks.name: Spending by the Veterans Affairs Health Care System for Medicare Advantage Enrollees

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**ApprovingOfficial**:
Matt Davio, ORD Communications Chief

**Attribution**: Amal Trivedi, MD, MPH, Investigator, THRIVE COIN at
the Providence VA Medical Center (Center of Innovation in Long-term Services
and Supports)

**Outlet**: U.S. Medicine

**Subject: **Spending by the Veterans Affairs Health Care System
for Medicare Advantage Enrollees** **

**Deadline: **1/8

**Background:** This query is requesting responses to questions about
an article in JAMA Health Forum called “Spending by the Veterans Affairs Health
Care System for Medicare Advantage Enrollees”: [https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama-health-forum/fullarticle/2842676?resultClick=1#250734311](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fjamanetwork.com%2Fjournals%2Fjama-health-forum%2Ffullarticle%2F2842676%3FresultClick%3D1%23250734311&data=05%7C02%7C%7C6e4aed0cfda044e84e2f08de4e3126f7%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C639034170166830311%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=H5HIobC8bpGmj%2Bfjiyjp8Jp%2FeZb48q6IyoGYJp%2F7FV8%3D&reserved=0)

Q1: **If you are/were aclinician, how would you use the results of this review in your practice?**

A1: As a clinician, I would
use these findings as a reminder that many Veterans are enrolled in multiple
health systems simultaneously, and that a substantial and growing share are
enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans. It raises the possibility of duplicative
federal spending and fragmented care when Veterans receive most or all of their
care through the VA while Medicare Advantage plans receive full capitated
payments. From a clinical standpoint, recognizing dual enrollment can help
improve care coordination, reduce unnecessary duplication of tests or services,
and ensure that care decisions are informed by a complete picture of the
patient’s healthcare use across systems.

Q2: **How do these resultschange practice for physicians?**

A2: The results don’t point
to a specific clinical intervention, but they highlight an important issue for
everyday care: dual enrollment in the VA and Medicare Advantage is now common
and growing. For physicians, this reinforces the importance of documenting
Veterans’ insurance coverage and understanding when care is being delivered
outside the VA, including through Medicare Advantage networks and community
providers. Better awareness of dual enrollment has the potential to reduce
duplication of services, improve communication across systems, and support
safer, more coordinated care for Veterans.

Q3: **What is the mostimportant finding(s) about this review that our audience (healthcareprofessionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military HealthSystem and the Indian Health Service) should know**?

A3: The most important
finding is the magnitude and rapid growth of VA spending on care for Veterans
enrolled in Medicare Advantage, reaching nearly $23 billion in 2023, or about
one-fifth of the VA’s healthcare budget. This occurs even though MA plans receive
full capitated payments from Medicare for these Veterans, even as VA finances
much or even all of their care. These patterns suggest substantial potential
for duplicative federal spending and raise important questions about how
Veterans’ care is financed and coordinated across programs.

Q4: **What situation causedyou to want to do this review?**

A4: We were motivated by
longstanding concerns that Medicare Advantage plans may benefit financially
when Veterans enroll but rely heavily on VA care, effectively shifting costs to
a separately funded federal health system. With MA enrollment among Veterans
increasing and bipartisan legislation now proposing to allow the VA to bill MA
plans, there was a clear need for updated estimates of how much care the VA
provides to MA-enrolled Veterans, and how this has changed in recent years.

Q5: **Is there anything thatyou’d like to add?**

A5: These findings highlight
a potential duplication in federal healthcare financing in which MA plans
receive full capitated payments while the VA often delivers substantial, or
even the entirety of, care for the same individuals. The VA is also paying for
a growing share of Veterans’ care in the community for those already accessing
care in the private sector through MA. As dual enrollment continues to grow,
addressing this potential duplication of spending will be important for
improving efficiency, transparency, and long-term sustainability while
continuing to prioritize high-quality care for Veterans.

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**BLUF:** VHA Media Relations received a US Medicine query
regarding *[The 21st century marks a rise in TURP retreatment rates: ananalysis of Veterans Health Administration data](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fpmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Farticles%2FPMC12628495%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7C5ce46784d5af448e0b3708de3116a085%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C639002170408516481%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cvk%2Fmw%2BPPS5jYBVWKKXeevBXfpUCWI%2Fg9L7B%2F%2BOG%2FUo%3D&reserved=0)*, which Dr. Tyler Sheetz
and Kylie Morgan, San Diego VAMC, are co-authors. **Would one of the authorsbe willing to respond to the below questions by COB 12/9?**

**AOs: **Bruce Friedland, ORD Communications

**Attribution: **Tyler J. Sheetz, Urologist, San Diego VAMC

** **

**Outlet**: US Medicine

**Subject: **TURP retreatment rates

**ORD Deadline: **12/9

** **

**Questions:**

**Topline:*** The responses are based onthe author’s opinions, and not that of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs(VA) or the U.S. government.*

** **

Q1: What led you to study trends in TURP outcomes, and why did you focus on VA patients?A1: TURP has been considered by some to be the ‘gold standard’ surgical treatment for enlarged prostates for decades, but newer procedures and medications may mean trainees might get less hands-on experience with traditional TURP. We focused on VA patients because the VA has a meticulously maintained databases such as VINCI  (VA Informatics and Computing Infrastructure) VINCI is a VA-wide platform and secure workspace that provides researchers access to integrated national VA electronic health record and related data. This allows us to follow long-term outcomes in a real-world population of Veterans.
 
Q2: Before analyzing the data, what did you expect to find about TURP outcomes over time?
A2: We were wondering if outcomes from the TURP procedure changed over time with the advent of other new medications and surgical procedures for BPH.
 
Q3: Your study found that modern TURPs are more likely to require a repeat procedure but have lower incontinence rates. What do you think explains this pattern?
A3: Modern-era patients were more often treated with BPH medications and had more alternative procedures available, which may make subsequent surgery more common. Prior work has suggested that evolving TURP techniques and case selection could affect durability and complications, but our data did not include operative details, so these factors cannot be confirmed in this analysis.
 
Q4: Were there any results that surprised you?
A4: We were surprised that while stress urinary incontinence (SUI) rates were the same in the historical vs modern cohorts, when we grouped all types of incontinence together we saw a significant difference between the two groups.
 
Q5: What are the implications of your findings -- will they guide urologists in counseling patients about TURP versus newer BPH treatments? Do these trends suggest changes in surgical technique or training might help improve long-term TURP success?
A5: Our results raise the question of whether the outcomes from TURP in the current era differ from those in prior eras, which could have implications for assessing the clinical role of the multiple surgical modalities currently available to manage BPH. We expect that TURP will continue to have a large role in BPH surgical management. While we show that results of TURP may have shifted over time, our study was not designed to assess the reason for this, and further work should be done to assess potential causes. The data can help frame discussions about expected retreatment and incontinence risks, but do not, by themselves, dictate a preferred procedure over newer BPH treatments.
 
Q6: With so many new BPH procedures available, where do you see TURP fitting into current clinical practice?
A6: We expect TURP will continue to play an important role as one of several surgical options for BPH, rather than as a universal ‘default’ procedure. The optimal approach is typically individualized, taking into account anatomy, prior medical therapy, and the specific benefits and risks of TURP compared with other available procedures.

tasks.name: Prevalence and Risk Factors of Cognitive Impairment in Pragmatic Comparative Effectiveness of Primary Care Treatments for Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**Outlet**: Physician’s Weekly

**Subject: **Request for an Email Interview with Dr. Fortney

**Received: **November 13, 2025

**Deadline:** Monday, November 17, 2025

**Q1: Why did you feel thistopic needed exploration?**

**A1:** There
have been very few comparative trials between psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy
for treating PTSD, and none of them were conducted under routine care
conditions in a primary care setting. This gap in research needed to be
addressed to understand how effective these treatments are in real-world
clinical practice, particularly in primary care where PTSD treatment options
need to be feasible and accessible.

**Q2: What are the challenges in treating patients with PTSD in the primarycare setting? **

**A2:** Treating
PTSD in primary care presents several challenges. Avoidance is a core symptom
of PTSD, making sustained engagement in trauma-focused therapy particularly
difficult. Primary care settings often do not have the resources for extensive
therapist training and long therapy sessions. Additionally, patients in primary
care might not be as prepared to engage in trauma-focused psychotherapy as
those in specialty mental health settings, requiring additional support to
maintain engagement in treatment.

**Q3: What are the mostimportant findings from your work?**

**A3**: One
of the key findings from our work is that both written exposure therapy (WET)
and SSRIs are effective treatments for PTSD in a primary care setting. However,
engagement and adherence rates differ significantly between these treatments,
with SSRIs having higher adherence rates. This suggests that treatment choices
in primary care should consider patient preferences and readiness to engage
with the therapy.

**Q4: How can these findingsbe incorporated into practice?**

**A4:** Primary
care providers should present both WET and SSRIs as treatment options to their
patients with PTSD, taking into account individual preferences and treatment
histories. Supports like motivational interviewing and collaborative care
should be integrated to help patients initiate and complete trauma-focused
psychotherapies. This patient-centered approach can improve treatment outcomes
and adherence.

**Q5: What makes this issueparticularly urgent in the healthcare landscape?**

**A5:** PTSD
remains a significant mental health issue, especially among Veterans. The
ability to provide effective and feasible treatment options in primary care is
urgent because it allows for broader, more accessible mental healthcare.
Addressing PTSD effectively in primary care can improve overall health outcomes
and quality of life for many patients.

**Q6: What would you likefuture research to be focused on? What still needs to be explored? **

**A6:** Future
research should explore the policies and strategies that can promote the uptake
of effective PTSD treatments in primary care. Additionally, research should
focus on how to best support Veterans in initiating and completing written
exposure therapy. Understanding the specific supports and systems that can
enhance treatment adherence and effectiveness in real-world settings is
crucial.

**Q7: Is there anything elsethat you feel *Physician's Weekly* readers would benefit fromknowing about your research?**

**A7:** It's
important for readers to understand that patient preference plays a significant
role in PTSD treatment outcomes. Our research underscores the importance of
presenting patients with options and involving them in the decision-making
process. By doing so, healthcare providers can improve patient engagement and
treatment effectiveness in primary care settings.

tasks.name: Association between post-discharge antibiotic use and C. difficile testing

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**ApprovingOfficial: **Matthew
Davio, ORD Communications

**Attribution:** Daniel Livorsi, MD,
Medical Director of Antimicrobial Stewardship, Iowa City VAMC

**Outlet:**U.S.
Medicine

**Background: **Reporter is requesting information regarding the study
titled, [Association between post discharge
antibiotic use and C. difficile testing as a surrogate for clinically
significant diarrhea](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fpmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Farticles%2FPMC12538357%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7Caf8b3c304bd049a68ce108de1c9425fc%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638979619771391410%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=uYhOJEfFCFrRBRT%2BjtEHQ6yacIocBfYf3BtwY06klGw%3D&reserved=0).

**Q1: What motivated you to focus specifically onantibiotic use ***after*** hospital discharge, rather than during hospitalization itself?**

**A1: We wanted to focus on this topic becauseantibiotics are commonly prescribed when patients are discharged from thehospital. In fact, one out of every five Veterans hospitalized in the VA isprescribed an antibiotic to take after discharge. Sometimes these antibioticsare unnecessary or given for a longer duration than needed. ** 

** **

**Q2: You used C. difficile testing as a surrogatemarker for clinically significant diarrhea. Can you explain why that measurewas chosen and what its limitations might be?**

**A2: Patients frequently develop diarrhea while onantibiotics, but at least within VA, this side effect is rarely recorded intothe medical record. We were therefore trying to come up with some other way ofcounting how often diarrhea occurs. We settled on the C. difficile test, a typeof diarrhea that can occur in people taking antibiotics who were recently inthe hospital. Testing for C. difficile is an imperfect marker of diarrhea, aswe acknowledge in the paper, but the use of this test was something that couldbe easily measured.** 

** **

**Q3: Were there particular types or classes ofantibiotics that appeared to drive most of the increased risk?**

**A3: The risk of C. difficile testing was slightlyhigher in Veterans who take certain antibiotics, specifically oralfluoroquinolones, oral clindamycin, and oral extended-spectrum cephalosporins.** 

** **

**Q4: Did your team find any differences in riskbased on patient factors—such as age, comorbidities, or indication forantibiotics?**

**A4: Other factors associated with a greater risk of C.difficile testing were inpatient antibiotic exposure, female sex, takingmedication to reduce stomach acid, taking medications that suppress the immunesystem, and having certain health conditions like inflammatory bowel disease,immunocompromising conditions such as lymphoma, leukemia, HIV, and organtransplants.**

** **

**Q5: About one in five hospitalized veteransreceived post-discharge antibiotics. In your view, how much of that prescribingis truly necessary?**

**A5: Based on prior studies, 40 to 80 percent ofantibiotics prescribed at discharge are either unnecessary or sub-optimal.Sub-optimal means that there might have been a better choice of antibioticsregarding type, dose, or duration of treatment.** 

** **

**Q6:What kinds of stewardship interventions ordecision-support tools could help clinicians safely reduce post-dischargeantibiotic use?**

**A6: This is still an ongoing area of research. Somehospitals have had success with pharmacists giving providers feedbackimmediately before discharge about how antibiotic use can be optimized at thistransition of care. However, this process can be time-consuming, and sometimespatients are discharged before these types of conversations can occur.** 

** **

**Q7. Were you surprised by the magnitude of riskincrease (40–56%)? What message would you most want hospitalists orprimary-care providers to take away from that finding?**

**A7: Overall, the risk was relatively low, as only onepercent of all patients who qualified for our study were tested for C.difficile during the 30 days after discharge. But you’re right that themagnitude of risk was 40 to 56 percent higher in those who were onpost-discharge antibiotics compared to those who were not. This was notsurprising to me. I would hope that it’s a reminder that we need to be carefuland thoughtful in how antibiotics are prescribed.** 

** **

**Q8: What are the next steps for this line ofresearch—are you planning to look at outcomes beyond C. difficile testing, suchas confirmed infections, readmissions, or mortality?**

**A8:** ** **Through our work, we are trying to understand how
VA hospitals can improve antibiotic-prescribing as Veterans are discharged from
these facilities. This transition of care has often been overlooked in prior
efforts to improve antibiotic-prescribing in hospitals. Our work will hopefully
help address this knowledge gap.

Deadline: 11/7
Questions: 

**Q1:What gap in PTSD treatment research prompted this trial? Why did you choosespecifically to conduct it in primary care?A1: **There have only been three efficacy
trials comparing psychotherapy to pharmacotherapy for treating PTSD, and none
of them were pragmatic effectiveness trials (i.e., conducted under routine care
conditions). Furthermore, none of those trials were conducted in the primary
care setting.

**Q2:Why did you choose written exposure therapy (WET) and SSRIs as the primarytreatment comparisons?A2: **Because we were conducting the trial in
primary care settings, the treatments had to be feasible to deliver in that
setting. SSRIs are a feasible treatment in primary care because primary care
providers are familiar with prescribing SSRIs to their patients with
depression, and there is good evidence that SSRIs are effective for treating
PTSD (in fact, two SSRIs are FDA approved for treating PTSD). Trauma-focused
psychotherapies like Prolonged Exposure therapy and Cognitive Processing
Therapy are known to be effective for treating PTSD, but they require extensive
therapist training/supervision and involve more and longer visits than is
usually permitted in primary care settings. WET has been shown to be
non-inferior to Prolonged Exposure therapy and Cognitive Processing Therapy but
requires far less therapist training/supervision and can be delivered in just
six 30-minute sessions.

**Q3:This was described as a pragmatic trial—what made it “pragmatic,” and how doesthat differ from a traditional efficacy trial? A3: **Efficacy trials are conducted under
ideal conditions (e.g., therapists are highly trained researchers, patients
with comorbidities are excluded). They generate evidence that a treatment *can*
be effective, but findings are not necessarily generalizable to everyday
clinical practice. Pragmatic effectiveness trials, on the other hand, are
conducted under routine care conditions (e.g., community providers and patients
with comorbidities). They generate evidence that a treatment is effective in
real-world treatment contexts and are highly generalizable to everyday clinical
practice.

**Q4:The study found both WET and SSRIs to be effective, but adherence rates werequite different. What do those engagement rates tell us about the practicalchallenges of delivering each treatment in primary care?A4: **Avoiding thinking about the traumatic
event(s) is a symptom of PTSD. Therefore, sustained engagement in
trauma-focused psychotherapy is a challenge because it is often perceived as
burdensome by patients due to the temporary distress associated with the exposure
element of the therapy sessions. Patients in specialty mental health treatment
settings may be more ready and willing to engage in trauma-focused
psychotherapies than those in primary care settings. Patients initiating
trauma-focused psychotherapy in primary care settings likely need more supports
to stay engaged in treatment.

**Q5:For patients who did not respond to SSRIs, switching to an SNRI seemed moreeffective than WET augmentation. What do you think explains this finding? Didyou find it surprising?A5: **That finding was surprising because we
had hypothesized that WET augmentation would be more effective than switching
to venlafaxine for patients who are not responding to an initial treatment with
an SSRI. It is difficult to predict which particular medication a patient will
respond to, and it often takes several tries to find the right medication or
the right dosage.

**Q6:Were there any patient characteristics—such as gender, trauma type, or baselineseverity—that seemed to predict who benefited most from psychotherapy versusmedication?A6: **We examined three characteristics that
we thought might make therapy less effective: gender, perceived access to care,
and cannabis use. We examined gender because previous trials of PTSD
psychotherapy have found that women experience greater symptom improvement than
men on average. We examined access because therapy requires more visits than
medications, and therefore therapy would theoretically be harder to engage in
because of the difficulty of attending sessions due to barriers like travel
time and out-of-pocket costs. We examined cannabis use because a previous trial
found that patients using cannabis were less likely to engage in therapy than
those not using cannabis.

**Q7:Based on these results, how should primary care clinicians approach first-linePTSD treatment decisions?A7: **For patients who are not ready,
willing, and able to accept a referral to specialty mental healthcare, primary
care providers should present both treatment options (SSRIs and WET) to their
patients and let them decide based on their preferences and treatment
histories.

**Q8:What do your findings suggest about integrating trauma-focused therapy intoroutine primary care—what supports or systems are needed to make that work?A8: **Supports such as motivational
interviewing and collaborative care may help primary care patients initiate and
complete trauma-focused psychotherapy. Motivational interviewing is
goal-oriented counseling designed to enhance a patient’s own commitment to
behavior change, in this case seeking treatment for their PTSD symptoms.
Collaborative care is a team-based approach that, among other things, provides
psychoeducation about treatments and promotes adherence to treatment.

**Q9:Many patients prefer one treatment type over another. How should patientpreference factor into treatment planning, given your findings?A9: **Patients in our trial rated the
acceptability of therapy higher than the acceptability of medications. Because
we did not find a difference in the effectiveness of initially treating
patients with WET or SSRIs, the choice of treatment should depend largely on
preference (in addition to which treatments have helped, or not helped, in the
past).

**Q10: What are the nextresearch questions that grew out of this trial? What’s ahead?**

**A10: **There are two
important avenues of future research. The first is to find out what policies
and strategies can be used to help promote the uptake of one or both of these
treatments in routine primary care settings. The second is to determine how we
can best help Veterans who are primary care patients initiate and complete WET.

tasks.name: Kidney Tubular Biomarkers Predict Risk of Death and Heart Failure Readmission in Acute Heart Failure

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
BLUF: VHA Media Relations received another request from U.S. Medicine regarding a research article in the Journal of the American Heart Association, Kidney Tubular Biomarkers Predict Risk of Death and Heart Failure Readmission in Acute Heart Failure. Would you please provide written responses NLT COB 10/8?

Approving Official: Bruce Friedland, ORD
Attribution: Nicholas W. Wettersten, MD; Cardiologist, Jennifer Moreno VA Medical Center in San Diego
Outlet: U.S. Medicine
Subject: Kidney Tubular Biomarkers and Acute Heart Failure

Q1: If you were a clinician, how would you use the results of this study in your practice?
A1: Although many of the biomarkers we studied are still in the research and development phase and not yet ready for immediate clinical use, our findings emphasize the importance of kidney tubule health in predicting outcomes in patients with acute heart failure. This is additional information beyond traditional measures of kidney function and cardiovascular disease. Traditional measures, such as serum creatinine, are commonly used to assess kidney function, but they may not fully capture the complexity of kidney health. Our study suggests that kidney tubule biomarkers could provide more nuanced insights into kidney health, potentially leading to improved prognostic accuracy for these patients.

Q2: How do these results change practice for VA/federal physicians?
A2: VA and federal physicians should stay updated on the progress of incorporating kidney tubule biomarkers into clinical practice. These biomarkers have the potential to provide valuable prognostic information for patients with acute heart failure.

Q3: What is the most important finding(s) about this study that our audience (health care professionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military Health System, and the Indian Health Service) should know?
A3: The key finding from our study is that kidney tubule health is often overlooked in clinical practice, yet it has significant prognostic value. Our study shows that assessing kidney tubule health can help predict the risk of death and heart failure readmission in patients with acute heart failure.

Q4: What situation caused you to want to do this study?
A4: As a heart failure cardiologist, I have often found it challenging to interpret changes in serum creatinine levels in my patients. We conducted this study to see if kidney tubule biomarkers could provide additional prognostic information beyond what serum creatinine alone can offer in cases of acute heart failure.

Q5: Is there anything that you’d like to add?
A5: Research increasingly indicates that changes in serum creatinine levels during acute heart failure events can have varying clinical implications based on the context. Kidney tubule biomarkers might become a valuable tool for clinicians to better understand and identify significant changes in serum creatinine in these patients.

tasks.name: Upper gastrointestinal symptoms and Gulf War Illness in a clinical cohort of US veterans: a retrospective, cross-sectional study

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
Outlet: U.S. Medicine
Subject: Upper gastrointestinal symptoms and Gulf War Illness
Received: September 29, 2025
Deadline: 12:00pm (EASTERN); Wednesday, October 8, 2025
Contact Info: Mary Anne Dunkin, madunkin@gmail.com, 404-272-1555.

Background: Reporter is requesting information from the authors regarding the findings in the following study: Upper gastrointestinal symptoms and Gulf War Illness in a clinical cohort of US veterans: a retrospective, cross-sectional study 
She is requesting a copy of the full study.

Q1: Much of the research on Gulf War Illness has focused on neurological symptoms, fatigue, and lower GI issues. What prompted you to turn the spotlight on upper GI symptoms in this study?

A1: Gulf War Veterans (GWVs) with Gulf War Illness (GWI) experience a wide range of symptoms. Gastrointestinal (GI) symptoms are commonly reported among these veterans. Although most GI symptoms reported are related to the lower GI tract, our results show that many GWVs also suffer from upper GI (UGI) symptoms such as dyspepsia, heartburn, nausea, and vomiting. Previous concerns about symptom management prompted us to investigate whether GWVs with GWI receive different evaluations compared to those without GWI. We were relieved to find that patients with UGI symptoms received similar care regardless of their GWI status.

Q2: You found that nearly all veterans with Gulf War Illness reported upper GI symptoms. Which of these symptoms stood out most to you, and what do you think their significance is?

A2: In our cohort, the symptoms that stood out most were “stomach pain” and “nausea or vomiting.” Approximately 89% of veterans reported stomach pain, and 80% reported nausea or vomiting. When these concerns are chronic or associated with other signs and symptoms, they could indicate a serious health issue and must be evaluated promptly.

Q3: Your study suggests that clinicians should be routinely screening for upper GI symptoms in veterans with GWI. What would you like frontline providers to take away from this research?

A3: We want frontline providers to routinely ask GWVs, especially those with a history of chronic symptoms, about their UGI symptoms. Both veterans and providers should be vigilant about changes in symptoms and the appearance of new symptoms. When GWVs report UGI symptoms, providers should thoroughly explore these symptoms, develop a list of potential causes, and create a plan for appropriate evaluation and follow-up. It’s important for providers to continue evaluating UGI symptoms as indicated, regardless of GWI status.

Q4: How do untreated upper GI symptoms affect the daily lives and overall well-being of Gulf War Veterans with GWI?

A4: Untreated UGI symptoms can significantly reduce the quality of life by increasing discomfort and stress in GWVs with GWI. Most people have experienced abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting occasionally, but imagine having those symptoms multiple times a week or even daily. Such chronic discomfort makes it hard to enjoy time with friends and family or perform well at work. Additionally, patients with persistent UGI symptoms might be concerned about underlying causes, especially if symptoms persist without an obvious explanation. Managing these symptoms often involves medications, which carry the risk of side effects, potential interactions with other medications, and increased out-of-pocket costs. 

Q5: The VA/DoD guidelines for chronic multi-symptom illness currently emphasize lower GI symptoms. How do your findings suggest these guidelines should be updated?

A5: The VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Chronic Multi-Symptom Illness (CMI CPG) currently focuses on lower GI symptoms consistent with Irritable Bowel Syndrome, chronic pain, and fatigue. However, it also advises providers to comprehensively assess all symptoms and treat according to the standards of care, which includes UGI symptoms. Our findings show that UGI symptoms are being managed appropriately for GWVs with UGI symptoms, regardless of GWI status. Given that the CMI CPG will be updated in fiscal year 2026, it’s important for the guideline revision team to consider our findings and include a more explicit focus on UGI symptoms.

Q6: What are the most important next steps to better understand and treat upper GI symptoms in Gulf War Illness?

A6: The most important next step is for clinicians to routinely ask GWVs about UGI symptoms and provide them with the necessary clinical care for evaluation and management. In terms of research, more studies are needed to understand why some GWVs experience persistent UGI symptoms even after standard evaluation and management. Developing new approaches to relieve the suffering of these veterans is crucial.

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
Approving Official(s): Bruce Friedland
Outlet: U.S. Medicine
Subject: Study in Journal of Affective Disorders on Self-directed Violence
Received: 8/26/2025
Deadline: 9/5/2025
Contact Info: LaTina Emerson, latina.emerson@yahoo.com, 678-521-9219
Background: Reporter is requesting information regarding an article in the Journal of Affective Disorders called “Associations of self-directed violence among traumatic stress correlates: Network analysis and comparison in veteran men and women”: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032725014594?via%3Dihub

Attribution
is to** Rachel Zelkowitz,** with National Center for PTSD, VA Boston
Healthcare System and Boston University Chobanian & Avedisian School of
Medicine 

**Q1: If you were a clinician, how would you use theresults of this study in your practice?** 

**A1: ** Many clinicians already check for depression and PTSD
when evaluating suicide risk, but our results emphasize the importance of
checking for both conditions to get a better understanding of suicide risk.
It's also important to assess for nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI), which is when
someone hurts themselves without intending to die, as this can also indicate a
higher risk for suicide. Our research looked at cross-sectional data (i.e., a
snapshot in time). However, in my clinical practice, I think about suicide risk
dynamically, monitoring symptoms during treatment and re-assessing suicide risk
as needed.  

** **

**Q2: How do these resultschange practice for VA/federal physicians? ** 

**A2:  **The VA already screens patients regularly for suicidal ideation,
depression, and PTSD, and this information is very valuable to physicians and
other mental health professionals to identify Veterans at risk. Our results
suggest that assessing for NSSI, particularly in patients with other mental
health symptoms, would also be beneficial. 

** **

**Q3: What is the most important finding(s) about thisstudy that our audience (healthcare professionals working in the Department ofVeterans Affairs, Military Health System and the Indian Health Service) shouldknow? ** 

**A3:  **I think one of the things that this study highlights
is the importance of assessing for NSSI in both men and women, in addition to
cognitive and emotional mental health symptoms. NSSI is defined as intentional
damage to one’s body *without* suicidal intent; however, our findings
showed consistent connections between this behavior and suicide-related
outcomes, even when we account for the impact of depressive and PTSD symptoms.
Other risky behaviors like disordered eating and substance use also seemed relevant
(in both men and women) but the patterns of connections were more variable.
Another important finding that emerged is that specific PTSD symptoms (i.e.,
negative alterations in mood and cognitions) were strongly related to
suicide-related outcomes in the networks. This may point to the value of
carefully assessing what types of PTSD symptoms are most severe. 

~~ ~~

**Q4: What situation causedyou to want to do this study? **

**A4:  **I am a clinical psychologist who is privileged to work
with women Veterans at high risk of suicide. I have consistently observed how
suicide-related thoughts and behaviors rarely occur in isolation, complex
presentations of negative thoughts, emotions, and behaviors are the rule, not
the exception. A very important goal in therapy is to understand how these
symptoms relate to each other for a given individual. Although women have
higher rates of PTSD, depression, and certain risky behaviors compared to men,
associations between these symptoms/behaviors and suicide are relevant to both
men and women. I wanted to identify which symptoms and behaviors were most
relevant to target in clinical practice. The finding about NSSI was
particularly striking. 

**Q5: Is there anythingthat you’d like to add?**** **

**A5: **Suicide
is a complex phenomenon, so taking a comprehensive approach to understand how
aspects of traumatic stress (also a complex phenomenon) impact risk is crucial.
It will be so important to keep studying how these factors play out for
individuals over time and over the course of treatment to see how we can
further enhance care. 

** **

tasks.name: Outcomes of Human Metapneumovirus Infections in Nursing Home Residents: A Matched Cohort Analysis

checklist.item: Rec'd questions and passed along to SME
Reviewed responses and sent to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
**Approving Official(s):** Bruce Friedland

**Outlet**: U.S. Medicine 

**Subject**: *The Journal of Infectious Diseases: Outcomes of HumanMetapneumovirus Infections in Nursing Home Residents: A Matched Cohort Analysis* 

**Received**:
8/26/2025 

**Deadline**:
12:00 PM ET 9/3/2025 

** **

**Questions: **

****

**Q1: If you were aclinician, how would you use the results of this study in your practice? **

A1: As a clinician,
differentiating respiratory infections from one another is difficult when the
primary symptoms of all infections are cough, fever, chills, and delirium. Any
respiratory infection is going to have higher morbidity and mortality in older
people who reside in nursing homes, so there are important consequences to
making a specific diagnosis.  Influenza and SARS-CoV-2 have treatments for
early detected infections.  So, we need laboratory testing to help
distinguish the virus types. Including human metapneumovirus (hMPV) infection
in laboratory testing helps to understand the pathogen of the current outbreak
and also manage facility prophylaxis. Despite its clinical importance, hMPV
remains underutilized in diagnostic testing and underreported in surveillance
data, leading to gaps in recognition of its role in respiratory outbreaks. hMPV
is the ‘newcomer’ to the respiratory infections.  I envision that
treatment and prophylaxis options will emerge in the coming years, making early
detection and reporting even more critical. 

**Q2: How do these resultschange practice for VA/federal physicians? **

A2: During our study, we
witnessed practice evolving as rapid respiratory test panels are increasingly
including hMPV.  This helps to identify the current outbreak, initiate
infection control practices including prophylaxis, and protect nursing home
residents. 

** **

**Q3: What is the mostimportant finding(s) about this study that our audience (health careprofessionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military HealthSystem and the Indian Health Service) should know? **

A3: hMPV has a similar risk
of hospitalization and death as influenza and respiratory syncytial virus
(RSV), in older adults and nursing home residents.  At the end of the day,
any respiratory virus is dangerous for a nursing home resident. Also, we found
that hMPV ‘season’ seems to follow as influenza season is tapering, in the
spring. 

**Q4: What situation causedyou to want to do this study? **

A4: As geriatricians, we are
trained to care and protect those who are vulnerable due to age, disability and
disease.  There is an ‘outsized effect’ of respiratory viruses in older
people with more death and disability from these infections.  Geriatricians
need to be vigilant and informed.  hMPV might be in 4th place among
prevalent respiratory viruses, but nature has shown us that hMPV too will have
its outbreak. Knowing hMPV exists in nursing homes, broadens our outlook of
addressing respiratory infection outbreaks.   

**Q5: Is there anything thatyou’d like to add? **

A5: The VA Community Living
Centers provides crucial information about respiratory infections in older
people across the geographic United States. The integration across sites, use
of a common medical record, laboratory standards, etc allow understanding of
respiratory infection patterns.  Harnessing that data could be a national
resource about the next respiratory infection outbreak. 

** **

tasks.name: Sex and race-ethnicity influences on opioid overdose deaths among veterans diagnosed with opioid use disorder
tasks.description:
**REQUEST:** Seeking information about study in Drug and Alcohol Dependence on Opioid Overdose Deaths Among Veterans

**OUTLET:** U.S. Medicine (a monthly publication that serves healthcare professionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military Health System, and the Indian Health Service) 

**JOURNALIST:** LaTina Emerson 

**DEADLINE:** 7/14

**STORY:** Article on the influence of sex and
race-ethnicity on opioid overdose deaths among veterans diagnosed with opioid
use disorder, based on the study "Sex and race-ethnicity influences on
opioid overdose deaths among veterans diagnosed with opioid use disorder
between 2016 and 2021" in Drug and Alcohol Dependence 

**BACKGROUND:** LaTina Emerson is a freelance writer working on an article for U.S. Medicine. She is seeking information about the study on opioid overdose deaths among veterans, particularly focusing on the influence of sex and race-ethnicity. The study is published in Drug and Alcohol Dependence and is titled "Sex and race-ethnicity influences on opioid overdose deaths among veterans diagnosed with opioid use disorder between 2016 and 2021."

**RECOMMENDATION:**Provide answers
to the following questions via email and share a PDF file of the paper.

1. **What did your study find     about the influence of sex and race-ethnicity on opioid overdose deaths     among veterans diagnosed with opioid use disorder between 2016 and 2021?**

Our study found that women and minority Veterans—particularly Black and Asian Veterans—experienced disproportionately higher rates of opioid overdose deaths compared to their White male counterparts. These disparities were especially pronounced** **for women** **during the COVID-19 pandemic, who also died younger than men
of opioid overdose. 

1. **Why     did you want to study the influence of sex and race-ethnicity on opioid     overdose deaths among veterans diagnosed with opioid use disorder?**

Previous research has largely focused on overall trends in opioid overdose deaths, often overlooking other factors that shape individual risk. We undertook this study to fill that gap and better understand how risks differ among different groups of people. 

1. **What is the most     important finding(s) about this study that our audience (healthcare     professionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military     Health System and the Indian Health Service) should know?**

The most important takeaway is that the burden of opioid overdose mortality is not equally distributed among veterans with OUD— women and minority Veterans face elevated and often hidden risk. Clinicians and systems of care can take these disparities into account to more effectively prevent overdose deaths. 

1. **What are your     recommendations to healthcare professionals who are treating veterans     diagnosed with opioid use disorder?**

     We recommend that healthcare professionals:

    1. Routinely assess how
      overdose risks affect different groups of people;
    2. Ensure access to
      medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD) for these groups;
    3. Tailor care
      approaches to address the unique needs of different demographic
      groups;
    4. Engage in proactive
      outreach during times of healthcare disruption (e.g., pandemics);
      and
    5. Partner with patients
      and communities to co-develop care models that are accessible,
      trusted, and effective.

1. **Is     there anything that you’d like to add?**

Yes. The opioid crisis continues to evolve, and so must our response. This study highlights the need to move beyond one-size-fits-all approaches and recognize that overdose risk is shaped by social and demographic context.
