Deadline: 11/18
Questions: 

We
are fact-checking social media claims that allege government waste. A [social post says](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fx.com%2Felonmusk%2Fstatus%2F1856665933537792272&data=05%7C02%7C%7C88871a20f13049c37a2208dd04c847ee%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638671980431447417%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=aqy3JhMUkUCQ09DzoVQCT22rmtwyP0%2B5bwanMymKo4w%3D&reserved=0) that the government spent
$4.5 million to spray alcoholic rats with bobcat urine. The dollar figure comes
from [Sen Rand Paul’s
2020 Festivus report](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.paul.senate.gov%2Fwp-content%2Fuploads%2F2023%2F02%2F2020FestivusReport.pdf&data=05%7C02%7C%7C88871a20f13049c37a2208dd04c847ee%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638671980431462920%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=YjqYMc4tF%2BHkSPw0sBywSBgNsh1wRWNseG9MHyJAfTA%3D&reserved=0) and stated that it was a VA/NIH project. 

Is
the $4.5 million figure correct? We did not find a document showing that
figure. 

Here
is what we found: the National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism[awarded $330,750
to Louisiana State University’s Health Sciences Center](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Freporter.nih.gov%2Fsearch%2F7y_5-ZmWAUGw6HKiTFJYsQ%2Fproject-details%2F10073019%23details&data=05%7C02%7C%7C88871a20f13049c37a2208dd04c847ee%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638671980431479906%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=6dUO3KPSS9h0TpJGWlTHnxilGf47LOmAze94de97je0%3D&reserved=0) to study post-traumatic
stress avoidance in rats when exposed to predator odor (bobcat urine) to better
understand how to treat PTSD in humans. The research project was funded two
additional times in fiscal year 2021 and 2022, with a separate one granted
$129,047 in fiscal year 2022, bringing the total to[over $1.1 million](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Freporter.nih.gov%2Fsearch%2FY7VpkYdwQkOsinUovlssag%2Fprojects&data=05%7C02%7C%7C88871a20f13049c37a2208dd04c847ee%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638671980431499783%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=oSExAN5MoXajGgGcfDQ9NEwMxs%2Fx74zQuwaJFPRHrfo%3D&reserved=0).

tasks.name: Long-Term Trajectories of Postoperative Recovery in Younger and Older Veterans

checklist.item: Review/rewrite and send responses to VHA Comms

tasks.description:
Topic: **Long-TermTrajectories of Postoperative Recovery in Younger and Older Veterans** 

Reporter name: Mary Anne Dunkin 

Outlet: US
Medicine 

SME: Dr. Daniel Hall 

Pertains to the following study: [https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamasurgery/fullarticle/2825246](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Furldefense.com%2Fv3%2F__https%3A%2Fjamanetwork.com%2Fjournals%2Fjamasurgery%2Ffullarticle%2F2825246__%3B!!NHLzug!JqRG9dMkUs9o-qgPhbD9sBMSGlzRAcvCikKUSerkXCe84cjFVZJuyQTQIwjvIgt7ffveg_ITCbLot7WNWW1COHcRcQ%24&data=05%7C02%7C%7Cc1bb41297b174e83de4f08dd04193518%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638671228493825001%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=XsBBv6acBSC5u9TwDroRejAgbYvaVrV3C08KsEr8PzU%3D&reserved=0)

Q1: What interested you in doing this study? Why was it important? 

A1: Our prior work showed that frail patients face a much higher risk of complications and mortality after surgery. While these outcomes are important, they don’t always align with what matters most to older patients, particularly those nearing the end of life. From our clinical experience, we knew that surgery sometimes marks the transition from independence to dependence on family or long-term care. However, this transition hadn’t been measured at a population level, which is what we aimed to address. Our findings now support more patient-centered discussions between surgeons and patients, especially on issues critical to Veterans’ quality of life, like maintaining independence. 

Q2: Was the purpose to understand postoperative recovery in veterans or what this population studied due to the availability of electronic health records? 

A2: The purpose of this study was to document, on a national scale, one significant challenge some patients face as they recover from surgery. Our analysis was possible because of the unique, comprehensive data resources within the VA, allowing for an in-depth study of this type. 

Q3: How did you go about conducting the study? 

A3: This study was supported by the Office of Research and Development, with generous assistance from the VA Surgical Quality Improvement Program (SQIP) and the Geriatric and Extended Care Data Analysis Center. We combined SQIP data with the Residential History File, which links information from Medicare, Medicaid, nursing homes, and other sources. This setup gave us day-to-day data on patients’ healthcare use before and after surgery, allowing us to analyze recovery paths and identify loss of independence at an unprecedented level of detail. 

Q4: Can you tell me what the Surgical Quality Improvement Program and Residential History File are? 

A4: The Surgical Quality Improvement Program (SQIP) is a VA-developed initiative that was later adopted by the private sector and embraced by the American College of Surgeons. Nurses systematically sample surgeries within a hospital system, collecting patient and care data from preoperative to postoperative stages. This data enables quarterly performance comparisons across hospitals, which has significantly reduced postoperative mortality over the past 30 years. However, SQIP was previously limited to outcomes within 30 days of surgery. We addressed this by linking SQIP with the Residential History File, which combines long-term datasets from Medicare, Medicaid, and nursing homes. This extended timeline lets us assess recovery and healthcare use over an entire year. What that means is that for a systematic sample of all surgeries in the VA, we had a reliable indicator of each person’s healthcare use inside and outside the VA for each day of the year before and after surgery. 

Q5: What were the key findings? 

A5: As expected, most patients recovered well within one to two months after surgery. This demonstrates the excellent outcomes Veterans deserve and expect. However, we also identified two smaller groups: one group that never regained independence, and another group with prolonged recovery, taking nine to 12 months. This data provides new insight into recovery patterns and potential long-term impacts on independence. 

Q6: Did any of these findings surprise you? 

A6: Yes, the group with a nine- to 12-month recovery trajectory was unexpected. We anticipated finding a group that lost independence, but the extended recovery period surprised us. This pattern couldn’t be detected in the first 30 days after surgery, as it only became evident through long-term data from the Residential History File. These insights can now guide patient discussions, helping set realistic expectations, including the possibility of a year-long recovery or potential loss of independence. 

Q7: What are the implications of your findings? How can this information be used? 

A7: Our findings can be used by surgeons and referring clinicians as they consider surgical treatment. They will help set realistic expectations for outcomes and inform a more robust process of shared decision making to ensure that the treatments provided are aligned with patient’s goals and values, especially regarding independence and quality of life. 

Q8: What is the most important point for our readers, most of whom are VA clinicians? 

A8: The VA has achieved strong surgical outcomes, which are often as good or better than those in the private sector. Because challenges to full recovery still exist for most patients though, our study helps paint a picture of what long term postoperative recovery can look like. Armed with this data, patients and surgeons can form more realistic expectations to determine if surgery aligns with the patient’s personal priorities, particularly if they highly value independence.

tasks.name: Psychosocial Well-Being at the Time of Trauma Exposure and Risk of PTSD
tasks.description:
Outlet: US Medicine

Reporter: LaTina Emerson
Contact info: latina.emerson@yahoo.com
SME: Dawne Vogt, Ph.D., of VA Boston Healthcare System

Study: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/article-abstract/2825351#249966938

**Questions**
Q1: What did your study find about the likelihood of individuals with higher pre-trauma vocational well-being, financial well-being and social well-being experiencing PTSD following traumatic events?A1:  Findings revealed that Veterans who were doing better with regard to their vocational, financial, and especially social circumstances at the time they experienced at a new trauma were less likely to develop posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). That is, Veterans who had jobs they were doing well in and found meaningful, had sufficient financial resources to meet their needs, and who reported having satisfying social relationships were less likely to develop PTSD when exposed to a traumatic event. This finding supports the potential value of well-being promotion efforts to reduce risk for PTSD among populations like the military Veterans that were the subject of this study.
  
Q2: Why did you want to study psychosocial well-being at the time of trauma exposure and risk of PTSD?
A2: Because most people will experience at least one traumatic event in their lifetime (at least 70%), it is important to identify factors that can increase or decrease their risk for developing PTSD, a mental health condition that can result from trauma exposure. Yet, to date most research has focused on identifying factors that increase rather than decrease risk for PTSD, and most of these factors are historical factors that are difficult to intervene on. In this study, my colleagues and I wanted to look at the question of whether Veterans’ broader well-being at the time of a traumatic event might influence their risk of developing PTSD, with the idea that if findings suggest that doing well in other aspects of one’s life is protective against PTSD (which is what we found), this would point to the value of greater investment in well-being promotion efforts for this population and potentially other populations as well.  
 
Q3: What is the most important finding(s) about this study that our audience (healthcare professionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military Health System and the Indian Health Service) should know?
A3: Much of the focus of support offered to Veterans in the health-care setting has been on treating disease rather than promoting well-being. This study highlights the need for an expanded focus on well-being, with the goal of preventing disease. This can be accomplished by identifying Veterans who would benefit from additional support to improve their vocational, financial, and social well-being and connecting them with supports as early as possible to avoid the development of PTSD and other negative outcomes.
 
Q4: What are your recommendations to healthcare professionals who are treating populations that are at high risk for trauma exposure, particularly those who serve in the military? Do you recommend giving these individuals access to resources that promote coping and trauma recovery?
A4: As noted above, this study points to the value of evaluating Veterans’ well-being with regarding to multiple aspects of their life circumstances and connecting Veterans who report poor well-being with the supports they need before these challenges can translate into greater risk for PTSD and other negative longer-term outcomes. 
 
Q5: Is there anything that you’d like to add?
A5: Happy to provide additional detail and context if it would be helpful to have a follow-up conversation.

tasks.name: Hearing Loss, Incident Parkinson Disease, and Treatment With Hearing Aids
tasks.description:
Reporter name: Mary Anne Dunkin

Outlet: US Medicine
Contact info: madunkin@gmail.com
SME: Lee E. Neilson, MD;
Pertains to the following study: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamaneurology/fullarticle/2824569

Q&A:

Q&A: 

Q1: What interested you in doing this study? Why was it important? 

A2: On the big picture level, we’re interested in identifying people with Parkinson’s disease as early as possible - the earlier the better. This is important because this time before symptoms set in or become profound is the time to intervene. The second reason to be interested in this study is that not only could we explore whether there was an association with hearing loss and getting Parkinson’s later in life, but we also knew that records existed of who had received a hearing aid after their hearing test. We therefore had a means to determine if receiving a hearing aid could modify – hopefully for the better! – the association with Parkinson’s. 

Q2: Had other studies looked at an association between hearing loss and Parkinson’s? If so, how was yours different? 

A2: There is a lot of work out there on loss of sense of smell and visual changes, but very little on hearing loss. In our paper, we cite the only 3 studies I am aware of exploring this association – one each from Taiwan, the UK, and Germany.  The first difference is that our study dwarfs the others in terms of number of people evaluated. We were able to analyze over 3.5 million US Veterans. This is over 3 times larger than the largest of 3 aforementioned studies.  The real important difference here, however, is that we relied on audiometry data, that is, instead of using a diagnostic code or relying on a participant’s subjective report – both of which are susceptible to significant error – we had a quantifiable measure of hearing loss.  This further allowed us to stratify by severity and determine if the degree of hearing loss had any impact on risk of PD. 

Q3: How did you go about conducting the study? 

A3: Once we had our questions laid out, we set out to answer this using data from the VA Corporate Data Warehouse (CDW), a nationwide relational database of electronic health record data. After settling on the right cohort of Veterans, we looked at their first audiogram and classified them as normal or abnormal hearing.  We then followed them forward in time to see what happened – did they get Parkinson’s? Are they still healthy? Did they die?  While the average follow-up time was 7 years, we had records for some people extending out to 20 years.  Then we asked whether there might be other systematic differences such as racial background or smoking status that could explain, or confound, our initial results.  Finally, we tried to validate our results using a few alternative approaches to lend more certainty to our initial findings. 

Q4: What was the importance of using VA data for the study? 

A4: I couldn’t imagine being able to do this study in any other population.  The VA has a large number of Veterans across the country with records extending back over 20 years. The VA has prioritized evaluating and intervening upon hearing complaints for a while now. So we can look at objective hearing loss, not merely a subjective complaint, and we can link those hearing tests with hearing aid prescriptions. That lower barrier to access for hearing aids within the VA was crucial to us investigating this association. We sometimes receive
questions about our use of VA data in research. However, the VA’s comprehensive
records and unique resources made it the ideal setting for this study.  I also hope that Veterans realize they might be contributing to research just by participating in healthcare at the VA, and they should be thanked. 

Q5: What were the key findings? 

A5: We had a few major findings: 1) Hearing loss is associated with a higher risk of developing PD later in life, independent of other known risk factors of PD; 2) Those Veterans with the most severe hearing loss had the highest risk of developing PD; 3) In those with hearing loss and at least one other condition known to be a risk factor of PD (such as constipation or orthostatic hypotension), their risk was synergistic (i.e. greater than simply adding the risks together); 4) Those Veterans who were given a hearing aid within 2 years of a hearing test had a reduced risk of developing PD. 

Q6: Did any of these findings surprise you? 

A6: We were quite surprised – but excited! – to find that if Veterans had documented evidence of receiving a hearing aid within 2 years of a hearing test, they had a lowered risk of PD later in life.  We can’t quite wrap our heads around why this might work, and biological plausibility is an important consideration when ascribing causality.  While we were delightfully surprised, there is more work to do to figure out how this intervention might work and if we can learn more about the precise groups who are going to most benefit. 

Q7: Would it be that hearing loss is an early sign of Parkinson’s or that hearing loss somehow contributes to the development of Parkinson’s? 

A7: This study cannot resolve this very insightful question. It is possible hearing loss could represent an early sign of PD akin to other clinical markers, like constipation. It is also possible that other changes occurring within the brains of
patients who develop Parkinson’s Disease make them more vulnerable, and an
added factor such as hearing loss could exacerbate the disease’s progression. We know hearing is important for social interaction and communication, and this sort of engagement is generally recommended for overall brain health. Additional studies looking at social interactions and exercise regimens would be welcomed. 

Q8: What are the implications of your findings? 

Q9: What is the most important point for our readers, most of whom are VA clinicians? 

A8/9: (*Response both question 8 and 9*): We want to underscore that correlation does not equal causation, but our findings that objective hearing loss is associated with later development of Parkinson’s disease is another study that adds to the mounting evidence that hearing is important to human health. We
trust that policymakers and others will consider these findings in light of all
available research, and that future studies will also examine this association
within the patient population. 

For clinicians, even the busiest of primary care providers, adding a brief hearing screen to the annual wellness visit, could improve quality of life for our Veterans now and in the future. This is especially important in the Veteran population because of the great access to audiology in this system which is not easily found in the community.

tasks.name: Recent Study on Fecal Microbiota Transplant for CDI (ContagionLive.com)
tasks.description:
Outlet:
ContagionLive.com 

Reporter: Sophia Abene <sabene@mjhlifesciences.com> 

Topic: Recent Study on Fecal Microbiota Transplant for CDI 
SME: **:** Aasma Shaukat & Dimitri Drekonja
Deadline: TBD

Q&A: 

Q: What specific limitations in your study design or execution do you believe may have influenced the findings of futility regarding FMT's effectiveness?A: We can only guess at the reasons for the results we observed. Some possibilities include how we defined who could join the study and what we considered a successful outcome, the number of prior infections participants had, the makeup of the fecal transplant (FMT), and the dose or method of giving it. One key point is that most participants tested positive for C. difficile using a PCR test, which might indicate the presence of the bacteria rather than an active infection. At the time we designed the study (and even now), both PCR and toxin tests are commonly used to diagnose C. difficile infections, and both are accepted in the U.S. We chose our primary outcome to reflect real-world conditions, looking at symptom recurrence with or without lab confirmation. However, this approach could have led to enrolling some people with other gastrointestinal issues that show similar symptoms. We also included participants with one or more recurrences of infection, and it’s possible FMT may work better for those with multiple recurrences. Lastly, while we used a well-established FMT product, there is no agreed-upon standard for the composition, dosage, or how it’s given. Other factors that could have affected results include the time between participants finishing antibiotics and receiving FMT or placebo (about 6 days for both groups) and allowing the use of certain medications like proton pump inhibitors, which are linked to higher recurrence rates.

Q: Are there plans to assess the long-term efficacy and safety of FMT beyond the 56-day endpoint to better understand recurrence rates?
A: Yes, the study was set up to track outcomes for six months to evaluate both how well FMT works and its safety. We've completed the analysis and are reviewing the results.

Q: Can you discuss any notable trends observed in the subgroup analysis based on the number of prior CDI recurrences, despite the lack of statistically significant differences?
A: The subgroups are small, so we can’t make any definite conclusions, but we did notice a trend where FMT seemed more effective in people with more previous recurrences and when it was given sooner after finishing antibiotics for the latest recurrence.

tasks.name: Hepatitis C Virus research

checklist.item: Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
Reporter name: Annette Boyle 
Outlet: US Medicine
Contact info: amboylebiz@gmail.com
SME: Dr. **David Ross**, **Dr. Timothy Morgan**

These
responses have ***not*** been cleared yet by the Specialty Care Program
Office – I’ve copied Dr. Anne Utech, Acting Deputy Chief Officer for SCPO. 

Q1: What was the calculation for the VA in
deciding to commit to treating every veteran with HCV in the system, given the
huge numbers and projected high cost? 

A1: It wasn’t a calculation – it was the right
thing to do. Then-Under Secretary for Health (and later Secretary of Veterans
Affairs) David Shulkin announced in March 2016 that VA would expand treatment
availability to all Veterans in VA care diagnosed with HCV, not just those with
advanced liver disease (ALD). This announcement was enthusiastically supported
by VA hepatitis C providers, facility directors, and directors of VHA’s
Veterans Integrated System Networks (VISNs) because of the following: 

- Veterans in VA care are
     disproportionately affected by HCV. HCV is three times more common among
     Veterans in care than in the general US population.
- Vietnam War-era Veterans have
     been particularly hard-hit by HCV. Many of these Veterans were born
     between 1945 and 1965, a period that overlaps with the birth years of the
     baby boom generation – the group in the general U.S. population at highest
     risk for HCV.
- Rates of hepatocellular
     carcinoma (HCC), cirrhosis of the liver, and liver failure were
     skyrocketing among Veterans in VA care with HCV.
- VA data and research showed
     that cure of HCV decreased death from all causes – not just liver disease
     – among all Veterans with HCV, not just those with ALD.
- The new direct-acting
     antivirals (DAAs) were much more effective (cure rates up to 95%) and much
     less toxic than standard treatments. They also shortened the time needed
     for treatment from 12 months of injections and pills to as little as 8
     weeks of taking one pill a day.
- At the time, Veterans in VA
     care living with HCV were 50 to 70 years old. If VA was going to do
     something to help these Veterans, it had to do something now.

Q2: How did the unique features of VA health
care make that decision possible and prudent? 

A2: VA was the only health care organization in
the U.S. with the structures and processes in place and the familiarity with
HCV to diagnose, link to care, treat, and cure over a hundred thousand patients
with this disease in such a short time. VA’s accomplishments in this
therapeutic area were due to:  

- The existing cadre of
     clinicians (both MD and non-MD) with enormous experience in diagnosing and
     treating HCV patients and equally enormous passion for treating and curing
     Veterans with HCV as soon as possible.
- A large cadre of VHA subject
     matter experts on HCV who willingly contributed their time and expertise
     to construct a system-wide approach.
- Identification of risk factors
     for HCV specific to Veterans in VA care, including Vietnam War-era
     service.
- Well-established, standardized
     clinical and laboratory guidelines for screening patients for HCV – VHA
     had already screened two-thirds of the Veterans in VA care for HCV by the
     time the DAAs became available.
- Early adoption of reflex HCV
     testing to distinguish chronic HCV infection from HCV exposure.
- Launch of VHA’s four Hepatitis
     C Resource Centers in 2000 to discover and disseminate best practices in
     HCV diagnosis and treatment, as well as education and training for
     Veterans and VHA providers.
- Integrating HCV care with
     treatment for alcohol use disorder, substance use disorder, and mental
     health conditions (an approach pioneered by VHA) that would otherwise be
     high barriers to HCV treatment.
- Access to state-of-the-art
     molecular laboratory testing within VHA, allowing providers in all VISNs
     to confirm HCV diagnoses, determine which strain (genotype) of HCV a
     Veteran had, monitor the effectiveness of treatment, and confirm cure.
- A uniform VA National Formulary
- Very close collaboration
     between clinicians and VHA’s Pharmacy Benefit Management Office at the
     VHA, VISN, and VA Medical Center levels.
- ***Extremely*** experienced
     and well-trained clinical pharmacists throughout VHA, with close
     interaction and collaboration between clinical pharmacists and other HCV
     clinicians at all VA Medical Centers.
- Interactive, system-wide
     coordination and communication by VHA’s National Viral Hepatitis Program
     and its National Hepatitis C Resource Center.
- Strong support by VA and VHA
     senior leadership
- Use of population health
     management methods, including clinical dashboards

- A versatile, robust
electronic health record, VistA (Veterans Health Information Systems
and Technology Architecture) that supported both individual patient care and
rapid construction of datasets allowing collection and analysis of actionable
information to improve access to HCV care at levels from VHA down to individual
CBOCs.

Q3: What was sacrificed to reach the VA's goal
for HCV treatment in terms of diversion of funds, staff, and attention? 

A3: This accomplishment required enormous
efforts on the part of field, clinicians, and staff, but it would be inaccurate
to say that resources were diverted or that other clinical needs were neglected
or sacrificed. The key was redesigning VHA’s HCV care systems to improve
access, quality, and value by  

- Emphasizing population health
     management by leveraging the capabilities of VistA, VA’s integrated EHR
     system.
- Organizing and redesigning HCV
     care at the level of Each VISN stood up an HCV Innovation Team (HIT), with
     each HIT charged with analyzing HCV care across their VISN, identifying
     gaps in diagnosis, linkage to care, and treatment, and developing and
     implementing ways of bridging those gaps. This effort was coordinated by
     VA’s National Hepatitis C Resource Center, led by Dr. Timothy Morgan,
     Chief of Liver Disease at the Long Beach VAMC, Rachel Gonzalez, and Dr.
     Angela Park.
- Developing VHA-wide standard
     guidelines for diagnosis and treatment.
- Training hundreds of non-MD
     providers, such as nurse practitioners and clinical pharmacists, as well
     as primary care physicians, to treat patients for hepatitis C, with cure
     rates similar to those achieved by liver disease specialists.
- Integrating HCV treatment with
     care for co-morbid conditions such as alcohol use disorder and substance
     use disorder (SUD) that frequently make treatment of hepatitis C,
     difficult or impossible.

More details are available in an article by Dr.
Pam Belperio, VHA’s National Public Health Clinical Pharmacist, and others,
published in the *Annals of Internal Medicine *in 2018, entitled “Best
Practices from the US Department of Veterans.” 

Q4: How has the success been maintained as new
Veterans have come into care? 

A4: New cases of hepatitis C are being
diagnosed among veterans in VA care because of the opioid use pandemic and
injection drug use. VA is addressing this new challenge by making harm
reduction services available at all VA healthcare facilities, including launch
of syringe service programs, to prevent new hepatitis C cases as well as
diagnose, link to care, and treat Veterans who have acquired hepatitis C. In
addition, VA has expanded its use of population health clinical dashboards,
allowing VA front-line providers to rapidly identify Veterans who haven’t been
tested yet for HCV, those who could benefit from repeat testing, and those who
have already been diagnosed with hepatitis C but have not yet been treated.
These dashboards allow VA clinicians to reach out to these Veterans and offer
them HCV testing and treatment, as well as referral for SUD or mental health
treatment services, if needed. 

Q5: Is an updated version available of
the Hepatitis C Trends in the VA chart that was used in the Clinical Liver
Disease article "HCV Elimination in the US Department of Veterans
Affairs?" 

A5: INPUT

Ungerleider dlynunger@yahoo.com

**Background:** The reporter writing a short
news article on Parkinson’s disease, based on your article, "[Post-Traumatic
Stress Disorder and Risk of Parkinson's Disease in a Veteran Cohort](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fpubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2F39177610%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7Cdda09f52d6b84adad43108dccc1fc16b%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638609683971048091%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=kMqbWwl7fj37PQ%2FXBBwymy31Myqj%2B%2BZqULb5H8hXjrA%3D&reserved=0),"
which was released August 20, 2024. 

**SME**: TBD (*Dr. Frances Weaver of Hines VAMC has an out-of-office message*)

** **

Q1: What are the clinical implications of these findings?

DRAFT A1: The findings of this study suggest that PTSD may be a significant risk factor for developing Parkinson's disease (PD) among Veterans. Clinically, this highlights the importance of monitoring individuals with PTSD for early signs of PD, particularly within the Veteran population. Given that stress-related mechanisms, such as inflammation and neuronal changes, are implicated in both conditions, integrating stress management and targeted therapies for PTSD might help mitigate the risk of PD. Clinicians should consider routine neurological evaluations and possibly early interventions for Veterans with a history of PTSD to delay or prevent the onset of PD.

Q2: What studies on this topic would you like to see conducted in the future?

DRAFT A2: Future studies should focus on exploring the underlying biological mechanisms linking PTSD and PD, particularly the role of chronic stress and inflammation. Longitudinal studies involving diverse populations beyond Veterans would help establish the generalizability of these findings. Additionally, research could investigate the effectiveness of early stress reduction and PTSD treatments in lowering PD risk. It would also be beneficial to examine the impact of co-occurring conditions, such as traumatic brain injury, on the relationship between PTSD and PD to develop more comprehensive risk profiles.

Q3: Is there anything else about this study or topic that you would like to emphasize to our readers?

DRAFT A3: One of the key strengths of this study is the rigorous case-finding method used to validate PD diagnoses, which enhances the reliability of the association observed between PTSD and PD. This study adds to the growing body of evidence that stress-related disorders like PTSD can have long-term neurodegenerative consequences. For Veterans and their healthcare providers, this underscores the need for heightened awareness and proactive management of PTSD, not only for mental health but also for its potential neurological impacts. Stress mitigation strategies could play a critical role in preventive care for populations at risk.

tasks.name: Effect of Starting Dialysis Versus Continuing Medical Management on Survival and Home Time in Older Adults With Kidney Failure: A Target Trial Emulation Study
tasks.description:
Outlet: U.S Medicine

Reporter name: LaTina Emerson
Contact info: latina.emerson@yahoo.com
SME: Manjula K. Tamura - Manjula.Tamura@va.gov

**Background:** *The reporter wanted writtenanswers to the questions for an article about a study in the Annals of InternalMedicine called “Effect of Starting Dialysis Versus Continuing MedicalManagement on Survival and Home Time in Older Adults With Kidney Failure: ATarget Trial Emulation Study”: [https://www.acpjournals.org/doi/10.7326/M23-3028](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.acpjournals.org%2Fdoi%2F10.7326%2FM23-3028&data=05%7C02%7C%7C6e529ef16aaf4a9dc0d508dccd03011c%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638610659995227388%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=WVy4selQvrmE69kStvDd9X8jKNMHSxjMKS8b2CRL89w%3D&reserved=0)* 

Questions: 

**Q1: What did yourstudy find about the effect of starting dialysis versus continuing medicalmanagement on survival and home time in older adults with kidney failure?** 

**A1:** The study focused on adults aged 65 or older with chronic kidney failure and an estimated GFR <12 ml/min/1.73m² who were not candidates for a transplant. We compared two scenarios: (1) starting dialysis within the next 30 days versus waiting at least 30 days with the possibility of starting later, and (2) starting dialysis within the next 30 days versus forgoing dialysis altogether and continuing medical management. We found that starting dialysis at this level of kidney function within the next 30 days resulted in modest gains in life expectancy but reduced time spent at home compared to both the group that waited at least 30 days and the group that chose to continue medical management without dialysis. The trade-offs between life expectancy and home time were more favorable for starting dialysis in subgroups with lower kidney function, more severe illness, or those who were older. 

**Q2: Why did you wantto study the effect of starting dialysis versus continuing medical managementin older adults with kidney failure?** 

**A2:** We conducted this study to guide decisions about whether and when to start dialysis in older adults, providing a context that helps patients understand the trade-offs involved. Measuring home time offers insight into the downstream effects of a complex and intensive treatment like dialysis. 

**Q3: What is the mostimportant finding(s) about this study that our audience (healthcareprofessionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military HealthSystem, and the Indian Health Service) should know?** 

**A3:** Observational studies like this one have significant limitations. The most crucial limitation is the potential differences between the group that started dialysis and the group that continued medical management, such as whether patients were experiencing symptoms of kidney failure—factors we could not fully account for. Ultimately, clinical trials are necessary to test these strategies and determine their effects on patient survival, quality of life, and symptoms. 

**Q4: What are yourrecommendations to healthcare professionals who are treating older adults withkidney failure?** 

**A4:** Engage patients in shared decision-making early in the process, and consider referring them to clinical trials that test decision aids, incremental dialysis starts, and symptom management strategies to strengthen the evidence base for managing chronic kidney failure. 

**Q5: Is there anythingthat you’d like to add?** 

**A5:** N/A

Potential SME: Dr. Felix W. Leung 

Q: What interested you in doing this study? What is your background?A: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), as one of the most widely used medications worldwide, are for treating conditions caused by excessive gastric acid. However, concerns are emerging about the extended and improper use of PPIs, which have been linked to a broad range of negative effects, including fractures and vitamin deficiencies. Our background is in clinical gastroenterology and gastroenterology research.

Q: Why was this study needed?
A: The widespread use of proton pump inhibitors was causing concern. Earlier studies indicated that PPIs could lead to reduced stomach acid and persistence of pathogens in the gut, possibly increasing the risk of respiratory infections. However, data on the association between PPI use and respiratory infections was still up for debate.

Q: What led you to believe that proton pump inhibitors would influence susceptibility to respiratory illnesses to start with? They don’t seem related.
A: It started from our own experience. Basically, when you took PPI, it would make your stomach less acidic, which could cause more bacteria to grow within your mouth and stomach. You know the mouth, stomach, and lungs were closely linked together. We were curious if by taking PPIs, these microbes might enter the lungs. A search of the literature revealed that in the past decade, there were reports connecting PPI use with respiratory infections. The pandemic also made us consider looking into how respiratory infections were connected in a larger cohort.

Q: Can you briefly describe how you went about conducting the study?
A: This study was mainly based on the UK Biobank cohort, which included over 150,000 participants at baseline. After retrieving the data, we performed multi-dimensional statistical analysis to assess the risk of respiratory infections under PPI use.

Q: Can you also describe the key findings?
A: PPI users were associated with increased risks of influenza, pneumonia, as well as COVID-19 severity and mortality compared to non-users. 

Q: Did any of the findings surprise you?
A: We were surprised that there was an increased risk of respiratory infections compared to H2RA users. This might suggest that the stronger the acid inhibition, the more the risk increased.

Q: Can you explain theories as to why PPI use would be associated with increased risk of influenza and pneumonia, as well as COVID-19 severity and mortality? Do you know if the same association exists for H2RAs?
A: When there was less stomach acid under PPI use, more microorganisms could grow in the oral cavity and gastrointestinal tract, potentially causing respiratory infections from micro-aspiration. In our future study, we can further compare the H2RA users with non-users to know if the same association exists for H2RAs. 

Q: What are the implications of your findings?
A: Since liberal use of PPIs is common, our findings could be crucial for preventing respiratory infections and reducing the misuse of PPIs. Clinicians also need to comprehensively evaluate the indications of PPIs.

Q: What is the most important message for our readers, most of whom are clinicians in the VA?
A: Suppressing gastric acid is simple, but appropriate control of gastric acid requires nuanced assessment for the benefits of patients. 

Ruijie Zeng
Hao Chen
Felix Leung

tasks.name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
tasks.description:
**Approving Official: *ORDINPUT* **

**Outlet**: *Gastroenterology & EndoscopyNews* 

**Subject: ***Inflammatory BowelDisease care* 

**Received: **August 20, 2024 

**Deadline: *August 27,2024; 12:00pm*** 

**Contact Info: ***SherreeGeyer sherreeg@cox.net*** **

**Background:** *The reporterwanted to speak to a SME about VA’s Inflammatory Bowel Disease care or receivewritten responses to the questions.*

Attributable to: **ShirleyCohen-Mekelburg**, M.D., M.S. 

Staff Physician and Research Scientist 

Director, Inflammatory Bowel Disease Program 
VA Ann Arbor Healthcare System

**Q1: Whydid you conduct this study?** 

A1: Telehealth care has been suggested as a way to improve access to
gastroenterologists, especially for patients needing specialized care like
those with IBD. The VA has led the way in offering outpatient care through
video and phone visits. However, some doctors worry about the quality and
potential risks of tele-visits compared to in-person care. So, we conducted this
study to see how tele-visits might affect IBD-related hospitalizations, using
it as a measure of potential negative outcomes. 

**Q2: Studyassociates tele-visit-delivered outpatient care with a 10 percent lower likelihoodof IBD-related hospitalizations. Why?** 

A2. The key takeaway from our study is that tele-visits increased exposure
to tele-visit delivered outpatient care is associated with similar risk of
IBD-related hospitalizations as standard, in-person care. In fact, tele-visits
might even offer some extra protection. This could be because tele-visits make
it easier to keep a close watch on patients, improve communication, help patients
be more involved in their care, and catch changes in health earlier. However,
further studies are needed to validate these findings. 

**Q3: Whywere OR visits associated with a 10 percent increase in tele-visit care? **

A3. Please see our response to Q2, above. 

**Q4: Whatimpact did COVID-19 pandemic have on IBD tele-visit care?** 

A4. This specific study didn’t focus on how tele-visit care changed during
the COVID-19 pandemic. This particular study was designed to look at the
effects of tele-visits on IBD-related hospitalizations, not at how the use of
tele-visits grew or evolved during the pandemic. 

**Q5: Thestudy cites limitations including use of electronic health record data, absenceof socio-economic and algorithms for assessing IBD severity. Do theselimitations require further study? If so, why?** 

A5. The limitations mentioned are common in studies that look back at
existing data. These kinds of studies are good for finding initial links
between things, like tele-visits and IBD hospitalizations, but they can’t prove
cause and effect. Also, they can be affected by other factors we didn’t
measure. There is certainly room for future prospective studies to validate our
findings and examine additional outcomes. 

**Q6: Howimportant is a hybrid or hub-and-spoke model to outpatient care of IBDpatients, especially those who require physical examinations? **

A6. This is an important question, and I am not sure we have an
evidence-based answer yet. here are initiatives both within and outside VA to
develop hub-and-spoke models of care that will support gastroenterologists in
providing comprehensive care to patients with complex specialty care needs. IBD
patients need different types of care, some of which can be provided remotely.
But others, like physical exams or procedures, must be done in person. We need
to better understand the most effective ways of providing IBD care using hybrid
models so that we can provide comprehensive care while also optimizing access
to IBD care. 

**Q7: Whatis the takeaway to gastroenterologists and IBD specialists? **

A7. Our findings suggest that both tele-visits and in-person visits are
effective options for managing IBD. This means that patients and healthcare
providers have some flexibility in choosing the type of care that best fits
their needs and circumstances.

tasks.name: Gulf War Illness

checklist.item: Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
***Note***: VA researchers are
not the authors of the study, but the reporter would like to get VA’s input.

Reporter name: LaTina Emerson
Outlet: US Medicine
Contact info: latina.emerson@yahoo.com
SME: Dr. Karen Block

**Questions**:

Q1:  A recent study published in PLOS One has discovered faulty cell function in veterans suffering from Gulf War Illness (GWI), also known as Gulf War Syndrome (GWS), is likely caused by intense exposure to hazardous biological and chemical agents during war service. The research revealed the cell structures integral for transporting calcium into cells, known as transient receptor potential ion channels, are defective in Veterans with GWI. Q1: What is your view of this study’s findings about the cause of GWI?A1: Overall, this is the first study to evaluate the activity of Transient Receptor Potential Melastatin-3 (TRPM3) in Natural Killer (NK) immune cells isolated from 6-controls or 6-Gulf War Veterans (GWV) with Gulf War illness (GWI). TRPM3 is a protein involved in sensing pain and inflammation, and the study found that its activity was reduced in these veterans compared to healthy controls.

However, the study has some limitations. For example, it didn’t include tests like gene or protein expression to confirm the reduced TRPM3 activity. There were also no animal studies or in-depth investigations into how this reduction might affect health. While the researchers have done similar work in other chronic illnesses like chronic fatigue syndrome and long COVID, this study doesn’t establish a clear link between TRPM3 and the symptoms of GWI.

Q2: Are these findings new/unique? A press release about the study refers to the findings as a “world-first discovery,” “landmark research” and “life-changing for the Gulf War Vets.”
A2: The conclusions drawn by the authors and the media are not supported by the data. More rigorous research is needed. Additionally, using TRPM3 as a biomarker for multi-symptom illness remains a far reach. It’s also not yet clear if TRPM3 could be used as a reliable biomarker for diagnosing or treating GWI, especially since reduced TRPM3 activity might be present in other chronic conditions as well. It isn’t clear if any of the GWV with GWI in this study were exposed to COVID or have a history of infectious diseases that give rise to long-term chronic multi-symptoms.

Q3: Do you think these findings could lead to a treatment for the often-debilitating symptoms of GWI?
A3: Possibly, but only when more evidence-based data is generated and validated. There’s still a significant gap in understanding how TRPM3 functions and how it could be targeted for treatment. The use of low-dose naltrexone (LDN) may be a novel approach for treating chronic widespread pain in GWV with GWI. LDN shows promise for treating for other chronic illness that exhibit widespread pain. However, the role of LDN inhibiting chronic pain or inflammation through TRPM3 is speculative.

Q4: Do you know approximately how many U.S. Veterans suffer from GWI? Do you know any hazardous agents they might have been exposed to?
A4: Current data estimates the GWI affects about a third of the nearly 700,000 men and women who served in the Persian Gulf during Operations Desert Shield and Desert Storm. 
Possible exposures include: Oil well fires, pesticides, Pyridostigmine Bromide, airborne hazards, vaccinations, burn pits, chemical agents, and/or other toxic chemicals and substances.

Q5: Is there anything that you’d like to add?
A5: Gulf War Illness and conditions like chronic fatigue syndrome are both complex, multi-symptom illnesses that we don’t fully understand yet. These two relatively mysterious illnesses were understudied until the pandemic known as COVID-19 spread throughout the world. Clinicians and researchers worked around the clock to delineate all aspects of this illness, including the severe inflammatory process that emerged in those infected and the chronic multi-symptom illnesses that was noted in a subset of patients, which is now known as long-COVID. 

Importantly, the amount of information generated from the medical field around acute SARS-CoV-2 infection to chronic long-COVID gave rise to new hope for understanding GWI and ME/CFS. We now have tools to evaluate novel underpinnings of these diseases with hope of developing new therapeutics for treatment. This manuscript is an example of new hope.

tasks.name: Race and Kidney Disease Research

checklist.item: Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
**Approving Official: ***BruceFriedland*** **

**Outlet**: *US Medicine* 

**Subject: ***Race and kidneydisease research* 

**Received: ***July 25, 2024* 

**Deadline: ***August 16, 2024* 

**Contact Info: ***AnnetteBoyle amboylebiz@gmail.com* 

**Background:** *The reporterwould like to discuss VA research race and kidney disease* **Recommendation:** *Provide answers to the questions/talking points for OPIAleadership review/approval.*

**SME/Attribution: **Dr. Cynthia Delgado

Q1: 
Why was race ever included in the eGFR calculation? 

A1:
The justification for the
inclusion of race in estimating equations stems from the fact that studies
showed that Black individuals tended to have higher serum creatinine levels.^1-3^ However,
the biological reasons behind this difference are not well understood, and it’s
unclear how this difference applies to other groups. 

Q2:
Was there resistance to eliminating race as a variable? 

A2:
There no resistance to
eliminating the variable. The challenge was in figuring out the best way to do
it, balancing social responsibility rigorous science.  

Q3:
What has been the historic impact of using race as a factor in eGFR
calculations on kidney disease outcomes and transplantation? 

A3:
 Some have asserted that the observed health and health
care disparities that disproportionately affect African Americans are
attributable to the inclusion of race in the kidney function estimation. However,
there’s no conclusive evidence that this is the main cause. Disparities in
kidney transplants existed before race was included in eGFR calculations and
are also influenced by factors like access to specialized kidney care, social
determinants of health, and genetics. 

Q4:
When did the VA stop using race in its calculations of eGFR? 

A4:
Under the leadership of
The Deputy National Executive Director of the VHA Kidney Medicine Program, Dr.
Paul Palevsky and The Executive Director of the VHA Kidney Medicine Program,
Dr. Susan Crowley, race was removed from kidney function estimation within
months of the publication of the recommendations from the National Kidney
Foundation and American Society of Nephrology (NKF-ASN) Task Force to reassess
the inclusion of race in diagnosis kidney disease.  The change was implemented very quickly. 

Q5:
Have you seen an impact from that change? 

A5:
Within months of the
publication of the NKF-ASN Task Force’s recommendations, the Organ Procurement
and Transplantation Network (OPTN) Board of Directors eliminated race-based
kidney function estimation from transplant listings, effective June 27, 2022,
and applied this change retroactively. This resulted in over 2,500 kidney
transplants and reduced wait times by about two years. 

More recently, OPTN has continued its
work by removing race from the Kidney Donor Risk Index (KDRI) calculation. The
KDRI is a mathematical formula that estimates the quality of a donated kidney.
Under the new equation without race, kidneys donated by individuals will be
graded by known biological parameters without the inclusion of a social
construct. With these changes kidney transplant is more equitable.  

Q6:
What else can be done to improve kidney care and access to kidney
transplantation for Black patients? 

A6:
Screening and frequently
rescreening patients who are at risk of kidney disease are of the utmost
importance. We also need to increase access to cystatin C testing to confirm
chronic kidney disease. Addressing the high cost of newer kidney function
preserving medications can increase access to treatments many individuals need.
For transplantation, we should be working together to streamline the process
for transplant evaluation and listing, currently this can take months if not
years to complete.

tasks.name: Onset of Irritable Bowel Syndrome, Dyspepsia, Diarrhea, Bloating, and Constipation in Deployed Gulf War Veterans

checklist.item: Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
Reporter name: LaTina EmersonOutlet: US Medicine
Contact info: latina.emerson@yahoo.com
SME: Dr. Qiqi Zhou, Sue A. Theus

Questions:

Q1: What did your study find about phenotypes of gastrointestinal symptom complexes in previously healthy Veterans who were deployed to the Persian Gulf?
A1: The study identified several gastrointestinal symptoms among previously healthy veterans who had been deployed to the Persian Gulf. The most common symptoms observed were diarrhea (reported by 48% of veterans), irritable bowel syndrome (21%), and dyspepsia (16%). Additionally, 11% of the veterans reported bloating, and 4% experienced constipation. These symptoms were not present before deployment and developed during or shortly after their time in the Persian Gulf. While not directly causal, this does suggest a strong association with these Veterans’ military service. These findings highlight the importance of recognizing and addressing these symptoms as a potential consequence of deployment.

Q2: Why did you want to study chronic gastrointestinal symptoms in deployed Gulf War Veterans?
A2: The motivation behind studying chronic gastrointestinal symptoms in Gulf War veterans stems from a longstanding recognition that military personnel frequently develop gastrointestinal complaints during and after deployment to conflict zones. Historically, such complaints have been documented among veterans from various military conflicts, making it an area of concern. Given the unique environmental exposures and stressors experienced by Gulf War veterans, there was a need to better understand the specific gastrointestinal symptoms they face. This study aimed to fill a gap in knowledge, particularly in understanding the long-term gastrointestinal health of these veterans and providing insights into how these symptoms could be managed.

Q3: What is the most important finding(s) about this study that our audience (healthcare professionals working in the Department of Veterans Affairs, Military Health System, and the Indian Health Service) should know?
A3: The key takeaway for healthcare professionals is the prevalence and persistence of gastrointestinal symptoms in veterans who have been deployed to the Persian Gulf. Given that symptoms such as diarrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, and dyspepsia were common and often persisted long after returning from deployment, it is crucial that healthcare providers routinely screen for these conditions. Early detection and management of these symptoms can significantly improve the quality of life for these veterans and may prevent the progression of chronic gastrointestinal disorders. Furthermore, the study underscores the importance of considering military service history when evaluating gastrointestinal complaints in veterans.

Q4: What are your recommendations to healthcare professionals who are treating veterans deployed to the Persian Gulf and are experiencing chronic gastrointestinal symptoms? Do you have any recommendations for future Veterans who may serve in the Persian Gulf and may be at high risk of developing chronic gastrointestinal symptoms during and/or after deployment?
A4: For healthcare professionals treating veterans who have been deployed to the Persian Gulf, it is recommended to incorporate routine screening for gastrointestinal symptoms as part of their standard care. Symptoms such as diarrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, dyspepsia, bloating, and constipation are common and can significantly impact a veteran's health and well-being if left untreated. Additionally, for future service members who may be deployed to the Persian Gulf, pre-deployment assessments should include a thorough evaluation of gastrointestinal health. This proactive approach can help identify and manage any pre-existing conditions that may be exacerbated by deployment, thereby reducing the risk of chronic gastrointestinal symptoms during and after their service.

tasks.name: Diversity and scale: Genetic architecture of 2068 traits in the VA Million Veteran Program

checklist.item: Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
**ApprovingOfficial: INPUT **

**Attribution:Scott Damrauer**

**Outlet**: U.S Medicine 

**Subject:**Genetic
architecture of 2068 traits in the VA Million Veteran Program 

**Received:**August 2, 2024 

**Deadline:*August 8, 2024; 12:00pm*** 

**ContactInfo: **Mary Anne
Dunkin madunkin@gmail.com 

**Background:** The reporter is writing an
article based on a study concerning your study Diversity and scale: Genetic
architecture of 2068 traits in the VA Million Veteran Program 

**Recommendation:** written responses

SME: Scott Damrauer. Sumitra Muralidhar, Jennifer Deen

** **

Q1:  Why did you decide to do this study? Why was it needed?A1:Typically scientists are studying in disease or health condition and investigating which, if any genetic variants are associated with it (genome-wide association study or GWAS). This is the first clue to understanding a possible genetic underpinning for that condition.  Alternatively, they are studying a single genetic variant and investigating what different health conditions or phenotypes it may be associated with (called a phenome-wide association study or PheWAS).

Our goal in conducting this large genome-wide phenome-wide association study of over 2000 health related traits and ~ 42 million genetic variants was to expedite discovery of genetic associations for a wide variety of health traits, subsequently leading to better understanding of their genetic basis.  The results of this analysis are being broadly shared with the scientific community at large, within and outside the VA. It was also our goal to find genetic associations in people of non-European ancestry, since most genetic studies do not include sufficient diversity in the population. MVP’s diversity allowed us to do this. Lastly, our goal was to leverage the data donated by Veterans enrolled in MVP to serve the larger good, which they intended it for.
The study also demonstrated the power of big data and the power of interagency collaboration (VA, DOE, NIH National Library of Medicine). A study like this is made possible by collaboration across multiple federal agencies and access to state-of-the-art technology. The data analyses for the study were conducted on two of the world’s largest supercomputers—DOE’s Summit and Andes—at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory in Tennessee. The primary analysis of over 2,000 traits and nearly 44 million genetic variants took over 14,000 GPU hours (equal to 14 days of wall time) for the supercomputers to complete, and resulted in more than 350 billion association analyses.
The outcomes of this study are universally available for further research through NIH’s National Library of Medicine. This will open the door for other top researchers to rapidly accelerate their own research on health concerns for minority populations.
 
Q2: Had any study like this been conducted before? 
A2: A similar study had been done by the UK Biobank, although it was with a smaller dataset comprised of individuals of European ancestry. 
 
Q3: How did you go about conducting the study? Would a study such as this have been possible without the Million Veteran Program?
A3: We analyzed the association of over 40 million DNA variants with 2000 different health and disease traits separately in individuals genetically similar to European, African, Admixed American, and East Asian reference populations in participants of the Million Veteran Program. The information regarding the participants DNA was obtained from a blood sample they donated when they enrolled in MVP; the information about the participants health and disease traits was obtained from their VA electronic health record which they allow secure access when joining MVP. Once we tested the associations, we used statistical approaches to identify the likely causal variants and genes, and to compare the genetics of the traits and diseases between the populations. 

This type of analysis was only possible within the VA Million Veteran Program. It is the largest and most diverse genomic and precision medicine cohort in the world. More importantly, this work was only possible because of the generous Veterans who have selflessly agreed to participate in MVP.

Q4: What were the key findings? Were there any specific genes in Veterans of non-European descent that were associated with disease or increased risk/severity of certain diseases? 
A4:  We identified 38,270 independent genetic variants associating with one or more health traits. Among these, 2,069 of the associations were found to be unique to participants of non-European populations, demonstrating the importance of expanding diversity in genetic studies. The study showed that the genetics of most traits are similar between groups of diverse people, but, certain groups do have their own distinct genetic features. We were able to find these differences more accurately, especially in African-American and mixed ancestry groups, thanks to improvements in how DNA is analyzed, like fine mapping, which allows researchers to identify the exact genetic changes responsible for a particular trait or condition. The findings highlight the importance of including diverse genetic backgrounds in understanding the genetic causes of health disparities.
Some of the key findings include:
•     Identification of 101 traits in Veterans with African ancestry that exhibit a prevalence at least twice as high as that observed in Veterans of European ancestry, including hereditary hemolytic anemias, sarcoidosis and keloid scarring.
•     Discovery of a new signal in Veterans with African ancestry associated with increased risk of gout.
•     Identification of new genes that are associated with alopecia based on increased prevalence of this condition in the Hispanic Veteran population.

Q5: Did any of the findings surprise you? 
A5: We were somewhat surprised by the degree of similarity in genetic architecture between populations.  There has been an emerging literature suggesting that there are more similarities than differences between populations. Our studies suggest that, in fact, there are actually only few differences in the genetics between populations. And when we do see differences they are largely due to the presence of different variants in different populations rather than different effects of the same variant in different populations.  It is still very important to understand both the similarities and differences as even small differences can have an impact on health and wellbeing.  

Q6: What are the implications of your findings?
A6: Our findings highlight the importance of including diverse participants in genetic studies. 

Q7: What is the main message for readers, most of whom are clinicians in the VA? 
A7: Genomic and precision medicine has the potential to dramatically improve how we care for our patients. Ensuring that we are able to do this equitably requires studying the genetics of health and disease in diverse populations. The VA MVP is leading the way in this endeavor. The results of our current work, and the many other completed and ongoing studies of MVP, is paving the way for improved care for Veterans and non-Veterans alike. The future of precision medicine will be powered by large studies like those that can be done using data from MVP. If you practice at a VA, please encourage your patients to participate in this important study either online at mvp.va.gov or at participating locations across the country.

tasks.name: Efficacy of a Pain Self-Management Intervention Tailored to People With HIV: A Randomized Clinical Trial

checklist.item: Rec'd concurrence from other relevant program offices
Received inquiry from VHA Media Relations

tasks.description:
**Reporter name**: Mary Anne Dunkin

**Outlet**: U.S Medicine 

**Subject**: **Efficacy of a Pain Self-Management Intervention Tailoredto People With HIV: A Randomized Clinical Trial - **https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2820966** **

**Received**: July 30, 2024 

**Deadline**: August 8, 2024; 12:00pm 

**Contact Info**: Mary Anne Jones madunkin@gmail.com 

**Background**: Reporter is requesting interviews; will accept written responses if interviews aren’t feasible 

SME: **Dr. Katie Fitzgerald Jones** 

Q: What interested you in doing this study? What is your background? 

A:  I am a Palliative and Addiction Nurse Practitioner with
nearly twenty years of clinical experience. My
research interests focus on the intersection between chronic pain, serious
illness (such as HIV and cancer), and opioid therapy. These interests are a direct
result of my clinical practice, where I witnessed a marked increase in
suffering from chronic pain among patients with serious illness who are living
longer often with side effects of life saving treatments.  

I became
interested in people with HIV through my mentor at the University of
Pittsburgh, Jessica Merlin, MD, PhD. People with HIV have high rates of chronic
pain, substance and opioid use, and limited access to non-pharmacologic
treatments tailored to their needs. Additionally, people with HIV share many
similarities to other populations that I routinely care for, such as cancer
survivors and older adults who face similar challenges with high prevalence
rates of chronic pain, opioid use, and communication issues with clinicians
because of stigma and ageism.  

Q: Why was this study needed? 

A: Although there is evidence that pain
self-management and peer support are essential to coping with chronic pain,
interventions are often difficult to access because they require psychologists
or other specialty-trained clinicians and are rarely tailored to the unique
needs of highly impacted populations. The STOMP intervention, created by the
study team led by Dr. Merlin, is delivered by staff already working in HIV
clinics and enhanced by peer support provided by people with HIV living with
chronic pain. STOMP is tailored to the needs of people with HIV based on
preliminary qualitative work.  

Q: Can you briefly describe STOMP? How is it used? Has it been used/studied in HIV patients before? 

A: STOMP combines one-on-one skill-building
sessions delivered by staff interventionists with group sessions co-led by peer
interventionists delivered over 12 weeks. All participants receiving STOMP were
provided with an initial one-on-one pain education session and then were asked
to choose 5 of 8 remaining sessions based on topics most interesting to them.
These topics were physical activity, weight loss, relaxation, sleep, building
self-worth, talking with friends and family about pain, and taking opioids.
STOMP was previously [pilot-tested](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpmc%2Farticles%2FPMC6047927%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7C03e6c75807be4e77c1f108dcb58e4874%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638584869910142880%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=K7fsr7Gn%2FJQNyUU8GSrUATupuewQiH9ATEvJ%2FGAfDPE%3D&reserved=0)
on people with HIV, and this study published in JAMA IM was a larger multisite
randomized controlled trial.  

Q: Can you briefly describe how you went about conducting the study? 

A: We published the STOMP
study protocol, which describes our study procedures. Due to COVID-19, we
had to pivot the STOMP intervention to phone-based 1:1 self-management skill
building and group peer support sessions. 

Staff and peer interventions
completed rigorous training on the STOMP intervention manual. We randomized 278
participants to STOMP (n=139) or enhanced usual care (n=139). The enhanced
usual care cohort received a copy and brief overview of the STOMP manual.
Participants randomized to STOMP were provided group sessions to discuss pain
self-management alternating with 1:1 sessions on pain self-management. There
were six possible groups and 1:1 sessions per 12-week block. We did not
interfere with routine care. Before and after the completion of STOMP,
participants completed study measures on pain, pain self-efficacy, and coping.
The primary outcome measure was the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), a 12-item
questionnaire that measures pain severity (BPI pain) and pain-related functional
interference (BPI-Interference) on a 0-10 scale with higher scores indicating
more pain severity or interference. 

Q: What were the key findings? 

A: Immediately post-intervention
(three months after enrollment), STOMP was associated with a statistically and
clinically significant improvement in pain-related functional interference. At
3- months post-intervention, these results were attenuated but remained
significantly improved among STOMP participants compared to enhanced usual
care. STOMP is among the first efficacious pain self-management interventions
specifically tailored to people with HIV. Additionally, although the VA has
pioneered the inclusion of[peers in pain self-management interventions](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpmc%2Farticles%2FPMC7728830%2F&data=05%7C02%7C%7C03e6c75807be4e77c1f108dcb58e4874%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638584869910153939%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=ThYIe2CyTf73PpWDyPe91qEUekGHVFyQdYqiEoNgXAI%3D&reserved=0), the results suggest STOMP may be more efficacious,
potentially because of the rigorous training, the combination of peer support
with 1:1 sessions, and peer compensation.  

Q: Were there any findings that surprised you? 

A: We are thrilled the STOMP intervention was effective
despite lower-than-anticipated adherence and were pleased to see it was
effective despite pivoting to entirely phone delivery because of the pandemic.
In the future, we hope to refine STOMP implementation further to determine the
most effective dose and mode of delivery.  

Q: What are the implications of your findings? 

A: We hope STOMP can be offered
in HIV clinics across the country, including at the VA. STOMP is easily
scalable since the staff delivering the pain self-management education are
clinicians (social workers and health educators) already core staff in HIV
clinics. Many of the investigators and I are practicing clinicians who witness
the suffering associated with chronic pain, and it is incredibly satisfying to
have developed and tested an intervention with such promise for improving
quality of life and pain management.  

Q: What is the most important message for our readers, most of whom are clinicians in the VA? 

A: The VHA is a leader in promoting individualized whole
person pain care. We are lucky to have pain-self management tools and peers
available through the [Whole
Health Systems](https://gcc02.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.va.gov%2FPAINMANAGEMENT%2FVeteran_Public%2FWholeHealth.asp&data=05%7C02%7C%7C03e6c75807be4e77c1f108dcb58e4874%7Ce95f1b23abaf45ee821db7ab251ab3bf%7C0%7C0%7C638584869910161616%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=TyhfodUhhcsehyiBYC9afitUCbFPupSRK0qgSjNs1rA%3D&reserved=0). Clinicians should leverage these resources to help Veterans
live better with chronic pain.
